> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Radiation Logging – Brief Description, Groundwater Movement, Water Table and Piezometric Level (Surface), Theory of Groundwater Flow, Darcy's Law and Its Experimental Verification, Differential Equation Governing Groundwater Flow, Groundwater Level Fluctuations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — RADIATION LOGGING, GROUNDWATER FLOW & FLUCTUATIONS

## 1.1 Radiation Logging — Brief Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Radiation Logging (Radioactive Well Logging)** is a geophysical borehole logging technique that uses the natural or induced emission of nuclear radiation (gamma rays and/or neutrons) to identify and evaluate the lithology, porosity, fluid content, and water-bearing properties of subsurface formations penetrated by a borehole.

**Two principal types used in Hydrogeology:**

**(a) Natural Gamma Ray Logging (GR Log)**
- A scintillation detector (commonly NaI crystal or Geiger-Müller tube) is lowered into the borehole.
- Measures **naturally emitted gamma radiation** from the radioactive decay of Potassium-40 (⁴⁰K), Thorium-232 (²³²Th), and Uranium-238 (²³⁸U) isotopes present in the formation.
- **Shales and clays** emit high gamma counts because they concentrate radioactive elements during weathering and ion exchange.
- **Clean quartz sands and carbonate aquifers** emit low gamma counts.
- **Use in Hydrogeology:** Identify permeable (water-bearing) zones vs. impermeable (clay/shale) layers; perform stratigraphic correlation between boreholes.

**(b) Neutron Logging**
- A radioactive source (typically Americium-Beryllium, Am-Be) emits high-energy fast neutrons into the formation.
- These neutrons collide elastically with hydrogen nuclei (which have nearly the same mass as a neutron) and lose energy rapidly, becoming **thermal (slow) neutrons**.
- A detector counts the returning thermal neutrons or secondary gamma rays produced by neutron capture.
- The **hydrogen index** of the formation controls the count rate.
- **In water-saturated formations** → abundant hydrogen → strong neutron moderation → **low count rate of returning fast neutrons** (i.e., high "porosity" reading).
- **Use in Hydrogeology:** Identify zones of high moisture content, distinguish between liquid-filled and gas-filled voids, locate the water table in some cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Radiation logging is a *qualitative + semi-quantitative* tool — it does not directly measure permeability, but combined with caliper and resistivity logs, it is extremely powerful for aquifer identification in Module 2.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a radiation log as a "stethoscope" placed inside a borehole. The formation itself whispers its secrets through invisible radioactive whispers. Clays are chatty (high gamma), clean sands are quiet (low gamma), and water molecules act like sponges that absorb the bullets (neutrons) we shoot into them. By listening carefully (counting the radiation), the hydrogeologist can "see" what the rock is made of without ever seeing the rock itself.

---

## 1.2 Groundwater Movement

> [!NOTE]
> **Groundwater Movement** is the subsurface flow of water through the interconnected void spaces (pores, fractures, karst conduits) of geological formations under the influence of gravity, pressure gradients, and hydraulic gradients.

**Modes of Groundwater Movement:**
1. **Intergranular (Porous Media) Flow** — through the interstices between grains in unconsolidated sediments (sands, gravels) and porous sedimentary rocks (sandstones).
2. **Fracture Flow** — through joints, faults, bedding planes, and fissures in hard rocks (basalts, granites, limestones).
3. **Karst/Conduit Flow** — through dissolved channels in soluble rocks (limestone, dolomite, gypsum); often turbulent (non-Darcy).

**Driving Force:** Difference in **hydraulic head** between two points — water always moves from high head to low head.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a tilted sandbox with marbles filling the bottom. If you pour water at the higher end, it trickles down through the gaps between marbles (intergranular flow). Now imagine a cracked concrete slab tilted the same way — water rushes through the cracks much faster (fracture flow). Same water, very different speeds, very different pathways. Darcy's Law, which we will study, formalizes this "trickle vs. rush" into a precise mathematical language.

---

## 1.3 Water Table and Piezometric Level (Surface)

> [!NOTE]
> **Water Table (WT):** In an unconfined aquifer, the water table is the surface at which the pore-water pressure is equal to **atmospheric pressure**. It is the upper surface of the zone of saturation where hydrostatic pressure equals zero gauge.

**Key properties of the Water Table:**
- It is a **free surface** — atmospheric pressure acts upon it.
- It fluctuates seasonally due to recharge (monsoon) and discharge (pumping, evapotranspiration).
- It generally mimics the topography of the land surface but with subdued relief.
- It is measured using a **piezometer** or observation well screened in the unconfined zone.

> [!NOTE]
> **Piezometric Surface (Potentiometric Surface):** In a confined aquifer, the piezometric surface is an **imaginary surface** to which water would rise in a tightly cased well penetrating the confined aquifer. The water rises to this level due to the **artesian pressure** in the aquifer.

**Piezometric Level (Head):** The elevation to which water rises in a piezometer tapping a confined aquifer at a given point.

**Key differences (KTU Board Favorite):**

| Feature | Water Table | Piezometric Surface |
|---|---|---|
| Aquifer type | Unconfined | Confined |
| Pressure at surface | Atmospheric | Greater than atmospheric |
| Well type | Open / unconfined well | Confined aquifer well |
| Source of rise | Gravity | Hydrostatic pressure of confining layer |
| Mimics topography? | Yes, subdued | No, can be above land surface |

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Picture a water-soaked sponge resting on a table — that surface is the water table. Now, if you take a drinking straw, push it into a sealed bottle of soda and seal the top with your thumb, when you release your thumb, soda rises up the straw above the bottle's mouth. That height is the piezometric level — the soda is "pushed" up by pressure trapped in the sealed bottle. Confined aquifers behave exactly like that sealed soda bottle.

---

## 1.4 Theory of Groundwater Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Theory of Groundwater Flow** is the framework of physical and mathematical principles that govern the movement of water through porous media. The classical theory is built upon three foundational pillars: **(1) Darcy's Law**, **(2) Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)**, and **(3) Equation of State / Compressibility**.

The flow is generally treated as **laminar** (slow, viscous) in porous media — this is the central assumption of Darcy's Law. For higher velocities (e.g., near wells, in karst), **non-Darcian (turbulent)** flow occurs, described by the Forchheimer equation.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Groundwater flow theory is like a budget: water comes in (recharge), goes out (discharge, pumping), and is stored (aquifer storage). The "rules of the budget" (Darcy's law) tell us *how fast* the money (water) moves between bank accounts (aquifer units). Combining the rules + the budget = the differential flow equation.

---

## 1.5 Darcy's Law and Its Experimental Verification (Preview)

> [!NOTE]
> **Darcy's Law (1856, Henry Darcy of Dijon, France):** The volumetric flow rate of water through a saturated porous medium is **directly proportional** to the hydraulic gradient and the cross-sectional area, and **inversely proportional** to the length of the flow path.

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot i$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric discharge $[\text{L}^3/\text{T}]$
- $K$ = hydraulic conductivity $[\text{L}/\text{T}]$
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to flow $[\text{L}^2]$
- $i = \dfrac{dh}{dL}$ = hydraulic gradient (dimensionless)

The full derivation and experimental verification are presented in **SECTION 3**.

---

## 1.6 Groundwater Level Fluctuations

> [!NOTE]
> **Groundwater Level Fluctuations** refer to the variations in the elevation of the water table (in unconfined aquifers) or piezometric surface (in confined aquifers) over time, caused by natural and anthropogenic factors.

**Causes (KTU high-yield list):**

1. **Recharge from precipitation** (monsoon infiltration) → raises water table.
2. **Pumping from wells** → lowers water table (cone of depression).
3. **Evapotranspiration** → lowers water table in shallow aquifers during daytime.
4. **Barometric pressure changes** → inversely affects water levels in confined aquifers.
5. **Tidal effects** → in coastal aquifers, water levels oscillate with ocean tides.
6. **Earthquakes / seismic events** → can cause sudden water-level changes (seismohydrological effects).
7. **River stage fluctuations** → bank storage effects in adjacent aquifers.
8. **Long-term climatic trends** → drought vs. wet periods cause decadal fluctuations.
9. **Artificial recharge** → raises water table.
10. **Loading/unloading by trains or heavy structures** → minor elastic responses.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the water table as a "breathing surface." It inhales (rises) when it rains, exhales (falls) when you pump, holds its breath when barometric pressure rises, and is gently rocked by tides if it lives near the coast. Long-term climate change is like aging — it slowly changes the lung capacity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Radiation Logging — Operational Steps

1. The radioactive source/detector probe is connected via a logging cable to a surface recording unit.
2. The probe is lowered into the borehole to the desired depth.
3. As the probe is hoisted up (typically at ~3 m/min for GR, ~6 m/min for Neutron), the detector continuously records radiation intensity.
4. The data is transmitted as a continuous trace on a log chart — depth on the y-axis, count rate (API units for GR) on the x-axis.
5. The hydrogeologist interprets the log to identify aquifers and aquitards.

**Key tool:** **API Unit (American Petroleum Institute Unit)** — the standardized unit for gamma-ray logs. 1 API ≈ 1/200 of the gamma-ray intensity of a standard reference concrete formation block in the API calibration pit at the University of Houston.

---

## 2.2 Hydraulic Head and Hydraulic Gradient

**Total Head ($h$)** at a point in an aquifer is defined as:

$$h = z + \psi + \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

In groundwater flow (low velocities), the kinetic energy term is negligible, so:

$$h = z + \psi$$

Where:
- $z$ = elevation head (datum reference) $[\text{L}]$
- $\psi$ = pressure head $[\text{L}]$
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity $[\text{L}/\text{T}^2]$

**Hydraulic Gradient ($i$):**

$$i = -\frac{dh}{dL} = -\frac{h_2 - h_1}{L}$$

The negative sign is introduced because flow occurs in the direction of *decreasing* head.

---

## 2.3 Darcy's Law — Complete Theoretical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation (Henry Darcy, 1856):**
Darcy packed a vertical sand column, supplied water at constant head, and measured the discharge $Q$. He found that $Q$ is:
- proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$;
- proportional to the head loss $h_1 - h_2$;
- proportional to a property of the sand (hydraulic conductivity $K$);
- inversely proportional to the flow length $L$.

**Step 2 — Discharge Form:**

$$Q = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Discharge (Darcy Flux) Form:**

$$q = \frac{Q}{A} = K \cdot i$$

Where $q$ has dimensions $[\text{L}/\text{T}]$ (e.g., m/day).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall to Remember:** Darcy flux $q$ is **NOT the actual pore-water velocity**. The actual seepage velocity $v_s$ is higher because flow occurs only through the interconnected pore area:

$$v_s = \frac{q}{n_e} = \frac{K \cdot i}{n_e}$$

Where $n_e$ = effective porosity (dimensionless).

**Step 4 — Three-Dimensional Darcy's Law (Vector Form):**

$$\vec{V} = -K \cdot \nabla h$$

In Cartesian coordinates:

$$V_x = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad V_y = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}, \quad V_z = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}$$

For anisotropic media with diagonal hydraulic conductivity tensor:

$$V_x = -K_{xx} \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad V_y = -K_{yy} \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}, \quad V_z = -K_{zz} \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}$$

---

## 2.4 Hydraulic Conductivity vs. Permeability

**Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$)** depends on both the medium **and** the fluid. **Intrinsic Permeability ($k$)** depends only on the medium.

$$K = \frac{k \cdot \rho_w \cdot g}{\mu} = \frac{k \cdot g}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $k$ = intrinsic permeability $[\text{L}^2]$
- $\rho_w$ = density of water $[\text{M}/\text{L}^3]$
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity of water $[\text{M}/(\text{L}\cdot\text{T})]$
- $\nu = \mu/\rho_w$ = kinematic viscosity $[\text{L}^2/\text{T}]$

At **20 °C** in pure water, $K \approx 10^7 \, k$ (when $K$ in m/s and $k$ in m²). This conversion factor is critical in KTU numerical problems.

---

## 2.5 ⚡ KTU FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot i$ | Darcy's Law (discharge) | $\text{L}^3/\text{T}$ |
| 2 | $q = K \cdot i$ | Darcy's Law (specific discharge) | $\text{L}/\text{T}$ |
| 3 | $v_s = \dfrac{K \cdot i}{n_e}$ | Actual seepage velocity | $\text{L}/\text{T}$ |
| 4 | $h = z + \psi$ | Total hydraulic head | $\text{L}$ |
| 5 | $i = -\dfrac{dh}{dL}$ | Hydraulic gradient | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T = K \cdot b$ | Transmissivity | $\text{L}^2/\text{T}$ |
| 7 | $S = S_s \cdot b$ | Storativity | dimensionless |
| 8 | $K = \dfrac{k \cdot \rho_w \cdot g}{\mu}$ | Conductivity-permeability relation | $\text{L}/\text{T}$ |
| 9 | $\nabla^2 h = 0$ | Laplace equation (steady-state, no recharge) | — |
| 10 | $\nabla^2 h = -\dfrac{W}{T}$ | Poisson equation (with recharge) | — |
| 11 | $\dfrac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2 h}{\partial z^2} = \dfrac{S}{T} \cdot \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ | Transient flow in confined aquifer | — |
| 12 | $i_c = \dfrac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}$ | Critical hydraulic gradient (quick condition) | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\Delta h = h_w - h_0$ | Drawdown | $\text{L}$ |
| 14 | $R = \Delta S \cdot S_y \cdot A$ | Groundwater storage change | $\text{L}^3$ |

**Where:**
- $b$ = aquifer thickness
- $T$ = transmissivity
- $S$ = storativity (storage coefficient)
- $S_s$ = specific storage
- $W$ = volumetric recharge rate per unit area
- $G_s$ = specific gravity of solids
- $e$ = void ratio
- $R$ = recharge
- $\Delta S$ = water-table rise
- $S_y$ = specific yield
- $A$ = area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions (KTU high-yield):**
> - **Dirichlet (1st kind):** Head specified: $h(x,y,z,t) = f(x,y,z,t)$
> - **Neumann (2nd kind):** Flux specified: $K \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial n} = q(x,y,z,t)$
> - **Cauchy/Robin (3rd kind):** Mixed (e.g., leakage through aquitard)
> - **No-flow boundary:** $\dfrac{\partial h}{\partial n} = 0$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of Groundwater Flow Theory

1. **Well-field design:** Predicting drawdowns and safe yields.
2. **Contaminant transport modeling:** Tracking plumes from landfills and industries.
3. **Land subsidence prediction:** In overexploited aquifers (e.g., Kerala's Kuttanad, Mexico City).
4. **Seawater intrusion control:** In coastal Kerala (Kochi, Alleppey, Kozhikode).
5. **Dewatering of construction sites:** Tunneling and basement excavation projects.
6. **Drainage design:** Agricultural and highway drainage.
7. **Dam foundation design:** Seepage analysis and piping checks.
8. **ASR (Aquifer Storage & Recovery):** Managed aquifer recharge programs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL WORK

## 3.1 Experimental Verification of Darcy's Law

**Setup (Darcy's Apparatus, 1856):**
A vertical transparent column of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is packed uniformly with sand. Two manometer taps measure water levels $h_1$ and $h_2$ at the inlet and outlet, separated by distance $L$. Water is supplied at the top; a steady-state discharge $Q$ is collected at the bottom in a measuring cylinder.

**Observation Data (KTU typical numerical):**

| Trial | $L$ (cm) | $A$ (cm²) | $h_1 - h_2$ (cm) | $Q$ (cm³/s) | $i$ | $q = Q/A$ (cm/s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 100 | 30 | 6.0 | 0.60 | 0.060 |
| 2 | 50 | 100 | 25 | 5.0 | 0.50 | 0.050 |
| 3 | 50 | 100 | 20 | 4.0 | 0.40 | 0.040 |
| 4 | 50 | 100 | 15 | 3.0 | 0.30 | 0.030 |
| 5 | 50 | 100 | 10 | 2.0 | 0.20 | 0.020 |

**Step-by-step Verification:**

$$\text{Step 1: Compute } i = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{L} \text{ for each trial.}$$

For trial 1: $i_1 = \dfrac{30}{50} = 0.60$

For trial 2: $i_2 = \dfrac{25}{50} = 0.50$

For trial 3: $i_3 = \dfrac{20}{50} = 0.40$

For trial 4: $i_4 = \dfrac{15}{50} = 0.30$

For trial 5: $i_5 = \dfrac{10}{50} = 0.20$

$$\text{Step 2: Compute } q = \frac{Q}{A} \text{ for each trial.}$$

For trial 1: $q_1 = \dfrac{6.0}{100} = 0.060$ cm/s

For trial 2: $q_2 = \dfrac{5.0}{100} = 0.050$ cm/s

For trial 3: $q_3 = \dfrac{4.0}{100} = 0.040$ cm/s

For trial 4: $q_4 = \dfrac{3.0}{100} = 0.030$ cm/s

For trial 5: $q_5 = \dfrac{2.0}{100} = 0.020$ cm/s

$$\text{Step 3: Verify that } q = K \cdot i \text{ (linear relationship with zero intercept).}$$

Compute the ratio $K = \dfrac{q}{i}$ for each trial:

$$K_1 = \frac{0.060}{0.60} = 0.100 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$K_2 = \frac{0.050}{0.50} = 0.100 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$K_3 = \frac{0.040}{0.40} = 0.100 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$K_4 = \frac{0.030}{0.30} = 0.100 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$K_5 = \frac{0.020}{0.20} = 0.100 \text{ cm/s}$$

**Conclusion:** $K$ is constant (= 0.100 cm/s for this sand) and $q$ is directly proportional to $i$. **Darcy's Law is verified.** ✓

A plot of $q$ vs. $i$ yields a **straight line passing through the origin** with slope $K$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limits of Validity of Darcy's Law:**
> - **Reynolds number range:** $1 < Re < 10$ approximately (laminar flow).
> - For very low gradients (clays), $q$ is non-linear due to electrostatic and electrochemical effects.
> - For very high gradients (gravels, karst, near wells), turbulent flow occurs and Darcy's law breaks down (use Forchheimer equation).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Differential Equation Governing Groundwater Flow (3D)

We derive the **3D transient groundwater flow equation** by combining **(1) Darcy's Law** and **(2) Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)**.

**Assumptions:**
- Homogeneous, anisotropic porous medium.
- Darcy's law is valid.
- Compressible fluid, slightly compressible porous matrix.
- Constant fluid density and viscosity in space (small variations in time only).

---

### **Step 1 — Control Volume**

Consider a representative elemental volume (REV) of dimensions $\Delta x \times \Delta y \times \Delta z$ located at point $(x, y, z)$ inside the saturated porous medium. Let the porosity be $n$ and the bulk volume be $V = \Delta x \, \Delta y \, \Delta z$.

---

### **Step 2 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)**

The general continuity equation in a control volume is:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho n)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of water $[\text{M}/\text{L}^3]$
- $\vec{V}$ = Darcy flux vector $[\text{L}/\text{T}]$
- $t$ = time $[\text{T}]$

**Conversion logic:** This equation states that the *rate of mass storage* inside the REV plus the *net rate of mass efflux* through the REV's faces must equal zero (no source/sink inside the REV).

---

### **Step 3 — Expand the Continuity Equation**

The divergence in 3D Cartesian coordinates:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho n)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho V_x)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho V_y)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho V_z)}{\partial z} = 0$$

---

### **Step 4 — Substitute Darcy's Law (Vector Form)**

$$\vec{V} = -\mathbf{K} \nabla h$$

For anisotropic medium with diagonal tensor:

$$V_x = -K_{xx} \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad V_y = -K_{yy} \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}, \quad V_z = -K_{zz} \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho n)}{\partial t} - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\rho K_{xx} \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\rho K_{yy} \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\rho K_{zz} \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right) = 0$$

---

### **Step 5 — Apply Compressibility and Storativity Relations**

**Water compressibility:**

$$\beta_w = \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial p}$$

**Aquifer (matrix) compressibility:**

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{1+n} \frac{\partial n}{\partial p}$$

**Specific storage:**

$$S_s = \rho_w g (\alpha + n \beta_w)$$

Where $g$ = gravitational acceleration. $S_s$ has units $[1/\text{L}]$ and represents the volume of water released per unit volume of aquifer per unit decline in head.

The storage term becomes:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho n)}{\partial t} = \rho S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

---

### **Step 6 — Final 3D Transient Flow Equation**

For constant-density water and constant $K$ in time (but possibly spatially varying):

$$K_{xx} \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + K_{yy} \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} + K_{zz} \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial z^2} = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

For **isotropic** medium ($K_{xx} = K_{yy} = K_{zz} = K$):

$$K \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial z^2} \right) = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

Or compactly:

$$K \nabla^2 h = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

Where $\nabla^2 = \dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial x^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial y^2} + \dfrac{\partial^2}{\partial z^2}$ is the Laplacian operator.

---

### **Step 7 — Confined Aquifer Form (with Transmissivity and Storativity)**

For a confined aquifer of thickness $b$, integrate over depth $b$. Using $T = K \cdot b$ and $S = S_s \cdot b$:

$$T \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \right) = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

Or:

$$\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = \frac{S}{T} \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

This is the **2D transient flow equation for a confined aquifer** (also called the **diffusivity equation**).

---

### **Step 8 — Steady-State Form (Laplace / Poisson Equation)**

For steady-state flow ($\partial h/\partial t = 0$):

$$\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

This is the **Laplace equation** (no source/sink) — applicable in regions far from pumping wells, with negligible recharge.

If there is a source/sink (e.g., recharge $W$ per unit area per unit time):

$$\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial z^2} = -\frac{W}{K}$$

This is the **Poisson equation**.

---

### **Step 9 — Radial Flow Form (Well Hydraulics)**

For flow to a well, transform to cylindrical coordinates $(r, \theta, z)$. Assuming radial symmetry and confined aquifer:

$$T \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial h}{\partial r} \right) = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

This is the **Theis equation** form, and its solution is the famous Theis (1935) drawdown formula.

---

## 3.3 Quick-Condition Derivation (Effective Stress Concept)

A classic KTU derivation. The **critical hydraulic gradient** $i_c$ at which quick (piping, heaving, boiling) occurs is found by setting effective stress to zero.

**Total stress** at depth $z$ below water table:

$$\sigma = \gamma_{sat} \cdot z$$

**Pore-water pressure** at depth $z$ (with downward flow of gradient $i$):

$$u = \gamma_w (z + i \cdot z) = \gamma_w z (1 + i)$$

**Effective stress** (Terzaghi's principle):

$$\sigma' = \sigma - u = \gamma_{sat} z - \gamma_w z (1 + i)$$

$$\sigma' = z \left[ \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w (1 + i) \right]$$

$$\sigma' = z \left[ \gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w - \gamma_w i \right]$$

Recall: $\gamma_{sat} - \gamma_w = \gamma' = \dfrac{G_s - 1}{1 + e} \cdot \gamma_w$ (submerged unit weight).

So:

$$\sigma' = z \left[ \gamma' - \gamma_w i \right]$$

**Quick condition:** $\sigma' = 0 \implies \gamma' = \gamma_w i_c$

$$i_c = \frac{\gamma'}{\gamma_w} = \frac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}$$

$$\boxed{i_c = \frac{G_s - 1}{1 + e}}$$

**Typical values:**
- For medium sand ($G_s = 2.65$, $e = 0.55$): $i_c \approx \dfrac{1.65}{1.55} \approx 1.06$
- This means a vertical hydraulic gradient > 1.0 will cause a sand bed to "boil" — a critical safety limit in excavation and cofferdam design.

---

## 3.4 Water-Table Fluctuation Method (Specific Yield Estimation)

A classic KTU problem: estimate specific yield from rainfall + water-table data.

$$\Delta S = S_y \cdot \Delta h \cdot A$$

Where:
- $\Delta S$ = volume of recharge $[\text{L}^3]$
- $S_y$ = specific yield
- $\Delta h$ = rise in water table $[\text{L}]$
- $A$ = area $[\text{L}^2]$

**Sample KTU problem:** A 5 km² watershed receives 250 mm of rainfall in one storm. If the water table rises uniformly by 1.0 m and 30% of the rainfall infiltrates to become recharge, estimate $S_y$.

**Solution:**

Infiltration (recharge depth) $= 0.30 \times 250 \text{ mm} = 75 \text{ mm} = 0.075 \text{ m}$

$$\Delta S = 0.075 \text{ m} \times 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2 = 375{,}000 \text{ m}^3$$

$$\Delta h = 1.0 \text{ m}, \quad A = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$S_y = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta h \cdot A} = \frac{375{,}000}{1.0 \times 5 \times 10^6} = 0.075$$

**Answer:** $S_y = 0.075$ (i.e., 7.5%) — typical for a fine-to-medium sand.

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Darcy flux $q$ with seepage velocity $v_s$** — they differ by the factor $n_e$. KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in $\vec{V} = -K \nabla h$** — flow is in the direction of *decreasing* head.
> 3. **Mixing up specific yield $S_y$ and specific retention $S_r$** — $S_y + S_r = n$ (porosity). $S_y$ is the drainable fraction, $S_r$ is the retained fraction.
> 4. **Using water-table equation for confined aquifers** — confined aquifers use *storativity* $S$ (10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³), unconfined aquifers use *specific yield* $S_y$ (0.05 to 0.30).
> 5. **Wrong units in Darcy numericals** — hydraulic gradient is dimensionless; $K$ has the same units as velocity.

---

## **PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between the Water Table and the Piezometric Surface.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Water Table | Piezometric Surface |
|---|---|---|
| Aquifer type | Unconfined aquifer | Confined aquifer |
| Pressure condition | Equal to atmospheric pressure | Greater than atmospheric pressure |
| Formation above | Unsaturated (vadose) zone | Confining aquitard (impermeable layer) |
| Measurement | Direct in an open well | Water rises in a piezometer cased into confined aquifer |
| Mimics topography | Yes, with subdued relief | No, can be above ground level |
| Fluctuation magnitude | Larger (meters to tens of meters) | Smaller (cm to meters) |

The water table is a *real* physical surface in the subsurface, whereas the piezometric surface is an *imaginary* surface representing the level to which water would rise in a well tapping the confined aquifer. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define Hydraulic Conductivity and Permeability. How are they related?**

**Model Answer:**

**Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$):** The volume of water that will flow through a unit cross-sectional area of a porous medium per unit time under a unit hydraulic gradient, at a specified temperature. It has units of velocity [L/T]. (1 mark)

**Intrinsic Permeability ($k$):** A property of the porous medium alone (independent of the fluid) that measures the ability of the medium to transmit fluids. It has units of area [L²] (often expressed in darcies or cm²). (1 mark)

**Relation:**

$$K = \frac{k \cdot \rho_w \cdot g}{\mu} = \frac{k \cdot g}{\nu}$$

Where $\rho_w$ is the density of water, $g$ is gravity, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity. (1 mark)

---

## **PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)**

### **Question A (14 Marks): Darcy's Law and Differential Flow Equation**

**(a) State and explain Darcy's Law. Derive the differential equation governing 3D transient groundwater flow in a confined aquifer. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** Darcy's Law states that for laminar flow through a saturated porous medium, the volumetric discharge $Q$ is directly proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$ and the hydraulic gradient $i$, and the proportionality constant is the hydraulic conductivity $K$:

$$Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dL} = K \cdot A \cdot i$$

The negative sign indicates flow from high head to low head. (1 mark)

**Darcy's Experimental Verification:** Already shown in Section 3.1. A plot of $Q$ vs. $\Delta h$ is a straight line through the origin. (Briefly: $Q = cA \cdot \Delta h / L$ where $c$ is a constant.) (1 mark)

**Derivation of 3D Confined Aquifer Transient Flow Equation:**

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation** in a control volume:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho n)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Darcy's Law** $\vec{V} = -K \nabla h$ (for isotropic, constant-density):

$$\frac{\partial(\rho n)}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(K \rho \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(K \rho \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(K \rho \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Storativity Relations.** Define specific storage:

$$S_s = \rho_w g (\alpha + n \beta_w)$$

Storage term becomes $\rho S_s \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t}$.

**Step 4 — Combine and integrate over aquifer thickness $b$:** Using $T = K b$ and $S = S_s b$:

$$T \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \right) = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

This is the **diffusivity equation** for a 2D confined aquifer. (5 marks distributed across the steps.)

---

**(b) A confined aquifer has transmissivity $T = 500 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$, storativity $S = 2 \times 10^{-4}$, and thickness $b = 25$ m. A pumping well extracts water at a steady rate of $Q_w = 2500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ for 30 days. (i) Calculate the hydraulic conductivity $K$ and specific storage $S_s$. (ii) If the drawdown at a distance of 100 m from the well after 30 days is 5.0 m, determine the drawdown at 200 m using the Theis approximation $s = \dfrac{Q_w}{4\pi T} \ln\left(\dfrac{2.25 T t}{r^2 S}\right)$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Computation of $K$ and $S_s$:** (3 marks)

$$K = \frac{T}{b} = \frac{500 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}}{25 \text{ m}} = 20 \text{ m}/\text{day}$$

$$S_s = \frac{S}{b} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{25 \text{ m}} = 8 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Drawdown Calculation at $r = 200$ m:** (4 marks)

First, compute the constant $\dfrac{Q_w}{4\pi T}$:

$$\frac{Q_w}{4\pi T} = \frac{2500}{4 \pi \times 500} = \frac{2500}{6283.19} = 0.3979 \text{ m}$$

For $r_1 = 100$ m, $t = 30$ days:

$$s_1 = 0.3979 \times \ln\left(\frac{2.25 \times 500 \times 30}{100^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}\right)$$

$$s_1 = 0.3979 \times \ln\left(\frac{33{,}750}{2}\right) = 0.3979 \times \ln(16{,}875)$$

$$s_1 = 0.3979 \times 9.733 = 3.873 \text{ m}$$

But given $s_1 = 5.0$ m at 100 m, we use the **Cooper-Jacob approximation** $s = \dfrac{Q_w}{4\pi T} \ln\left(\dfrac{2.25 T t}{r^2 S}\right)$. The ratio of drawdowns at two radii:

$$\frac{s_2}{s_1} = \frac{\ln(A/r_2^2)}{\ln(A/r_1^2)}$$

Let $A = \dfrac{2.25 T t}{S} = \dfrac{2.25 \times 500 \times 30}{2 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.6875 \times 10^{8} \text{ m}^2$

For $r_1 = 100$ m: $\ln(A/r_1^2) = \ln(1.6875 \times 10^8 / 10{,}000) = \ln(16{,}875) = 9.733$

For $r_2 = 200$ m: $\ln(A/r_2^2) = \ln(1.6875 \times 10^8 / 40{,}000) = \ln(4218.75) = 8.348$

$$\frac{s_2}{s_1} = \frac{8.348}{9.733} = 0.8576$$

$$s_2 = 0.8576 \times 5.0 = 4.29 \text{ m}$$

**Answer:** Drawdown at 200 m $\approx 4.29$ m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Radiation Logging and Water-Table Fluctuations**

**(a) Describe the principle and applications of Natural Gamma Ray and Neutron Logging in hydrogeological investigations. How do you identify an aquifer using these logs? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Natural Gamma Ray (GR) Logging:**

**Principle:** Potassium-40, Thorium-232, and Uranium-238 in rocks emit gamma rays. A scintillation detector (NaI crystal + photomultiplier) in the borehole probe counts the gamma radiation and records it as a function of depth. (1 mark)

**Log Response:**
- **High GR reading** → Shales, clays, organic-rich rocks (concentrate radioactive elements).
- **Low GR reading** → Clean sandstones, quartzites, limestones, dolomites, evaporites. (1 mark)

**Neutron Logging:**

**Principle:** An Am-Be source emits fast neutrons (energy ~4-14 MeV). They collide with hydrogen atoms in the formation, lose energy, and become thermal (slow) neutrons. A detector (usually He-3) counts the returning thermal neutrons, or a separate detector measures secondary capture gamma rays. (1 mark)

**Log Response:**
- **High hydrogen content** (water, oil, gas) → strong neutron moderation → low count of fast neutrons returning → high apparent "porosity" reading.
- **Low hydrogen** (dry, gas-filled, or low-porosity formations) → weak moderation → high count of fast neutrons returning. (1 mark)

**Identification of an Aquifer:**

| Log Type | Aquifer Signature | Non-Aquifer Signature |
|---|---|---|
| Gamma Ray | Low GR (clean, clay-free) | High GR (shaly, clayey) |
| Neutron | High neutron porosity index (water-saturated) | Low neutron porosity (dry or gas) |

A good aquifer appears as a **low-GR, high-neutron-porosity** interval on the combined log. (1 mark)

**Applications in Hydrogeology:**
1. Lithology identification and stratigraphic correlation.
2. Aquifer vs. aquitard differentiation.
3. Porosity (qualitative) estimation.
4. Bed thickness and depth determination.
5. Borehole-to-borehole correlation.
6. Identification of fractured zones (when combined with caliper).
7. Detection of radioactive contamination (U, Th anomalies). (2 marks distributed.)

---

**(b) Discuss the various causes of water-table fluctuations. A small unconfined aquifer in Kerala has a specific yield of 0.12 and an area of 8 km². If the water table rises by 1.5 m during the SW monsoon and falls by 0.9 m during the post-monsoon dry period, compute (i) the volume of recharge during the monsoon, and (ii) the volume of groundwater discharged (lost) during the dry period. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Causes of Water-Table Fluctuations:**

**Natural causes:**
1. **Precipitation and recharge** (SW & NE monsoons in Kerala) — primary cause of rise.
2. **Evapotranspiration** — lowers WT during hot dry periods.
3. **Tidal effects** — diurnal/semi-diurnal fluctuations in coastal aquifers of Kochi, Kozhikode.
4. **Barometric pressure** — inversely correlated in confined aquifers; small in unconfined.
5. **River/flood stage** — bank storage in floodplains.
6. **Seismic activity** — coseismic changes.

**Anthropogenic causes:**
7. **Pumping** — lowers WT and creates cones of depression.
8. **Artificial recharge** — raises WT.
9. **Irrigation return flow** — can raise shallow WT.
10. **Urbanization** (impervious surfaces) — reduces recharge.
11. **Land use change** — deforestation, afforestation. (3 marks for causes, ~1 page bullet form)

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $S_y = 0.12$, $A = 8 \text{ km}^2 = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$.

**(i) Volume of Recharge during Monsoon (rise of 1.5 m):** (2 marks)

$$R = S_y \cdot \Delta h \cdot A = 0.12 \times 1.5 \text{ m} \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$R = 0.18 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 1.44 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Answer:** Recharge volume $= 1.44 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 = 1.44 \text{ million m}^3$ (≈ 1.44 MCM).

**(ii) Volume of Discharge during Dry Period (fall of 0.9 m):** (2 marks)

$$D = S_y \cdot \Delta h \cdot A = 0.12 \times 0.9 \text{ m} \times 8 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^2$$

$$D = 0.108 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 0.864 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3$$

**Answer:** Discharge volume $= 0.864 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 = 0.864 \text{ million m}^3$ (≈ 0.864 MCM).

**Net Annual Storage Gain:** $R - D = 1.44 - 0.864 = 0.576 \text{ MCM}$. (Indicates a small positive water balance — sustainable management is needed to maintain this.) (Encouraged as discussion; not mandatory.)

---

## **TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 — Key Takeaways (one-glance revision):**

- **Radiation Logging:** Natural Gamma Ray Log uses naturally emitted $\gamma$-rays → **Low GR = Aquifer, High GR = Aquitard**; Neutron Log uses Am-Be source and detects hydrogen → **High H-index = Water-saturated zone**.
- **Water Table:** Free surface in an unconfined aquifer, atmospheric pressure; **mimics topography** with subdued relief.
- **Piezometric Surface:** Imaginary surface in confined aquifer; water rises **above** the aquifer top due to artesian pressure.
- **Darcy's Law:** $Q = K A i$ (or $\vec{V} = -K \nabla h$); valid for **laminar** flow only; **verified** by linear $q$–$i$ plot.
- **Hydraulic Conductivity ($K$):** $[\text{L/T}]$, depends on both medium and fluid; **Intrinsic Permeability ($k$):** $[\text{L}^2]$, depends on medium only.
- **Seepage velocity $v_s = q/n_e$**, NOT $q$.
- **Transmissivity $T = K b$**, **Storativity $S = S_s b$**, **Specific Yield $S_y$** is for unconfined aquifers.
- **3D Transient Flow Equation:** $K \nabla^2 h = S_s \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ → reduces to **Laplace** ($\nabla^2 h = 0$) for steady state without source.
- **2D Confined Diffusivity Equation:** $T \nabla^2 h = S \dfrac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ — foundation of Theis well hydraulics.
- **Critical Hydraulic Gradient:** $i_c = (G_s - 1)/(1+e)$ — beyond this, "quick condition" / sand boiling occurs.
- **Water-Table Fluctuation Causes:** Recharge (monsoon), pumping, ET, tides, barometric pressure, river stage, seismicity, land-use change.
- **Recharge formula:** $R = S_y \cdot \Delta h \cdot A$; **Same formula** for discharge estimation during drawdown.
- **Theis approximation:** $s(r,t) = \dfrac{Q_w}{4\pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.25 T t}{r^2 S}\right)$ — assume $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4 T t} \le 0.05$.
- **Boundary conditions:** Dirichlet (prescribed head), Neumann (prescribed flux), Cauchy (mixed), No-flow ($\partial h / \partial n = 0$).
- **Quick numerical sanity checks:** $K$ (sand) ≈ 1–100 m/day; $K$ (clay) ≈ 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁴ m/day; $K$ (gravel) ≈ 100–1000 m/day.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hill Diagram (Hill-Piper Trilinear Diagram)** is a fundamental graphical technique in hydrochemistry used to interpret groundwater quality data by plotting the relative proportions (in milliequivalents per litre, **meq/L**) of the three dominant cations ($Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$, $Na^+ + K^+$) and three dominant anions ($HCO_3^- + CO_3^{2-}$, $SO_4^{2-}$, $Cl^-$) on two separate triangular fields, with the results combined on a central diamond-shaped field. It is used to classify water into **hydrochemical facies** that reveal the geochemical evolution, recharge history, and flow path of groundwater.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This method is treated as a compulsory Part-B (14-mark) topic in Module 3. Students are expected to plot a given analysis, identify the water type, and interpret the geochemical significance (recharge vs. base flow vs. mixing).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Recipe Triangle"

Imagine you are baking a cake and you must report the **proportion of each ingredient** (flour, sugar, butter), not the absolute weight. Two cakes with very different absolute weights can have the *same recipe* (same proportions). 

Similarly, the Hill diagram ignores **how much** salt is dissolved (which is given by TDS) and instead tells you **what kind of dissolved chemistry** the water has — the "recipe" of dissolved ions. Two waters from different depths with different TDS values can be plotted and quickly compared by their dominant ion signature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three axes?** Because in natural groundwater, almost all dissolved load is contributed by the six major ions mentioned above. Everything else (iron, nitrate, fluoride) is treated as trace and not used in the diagram.

## 1.3 Underlying Geochemical Principle

The relative dominance of ions is governed by the **sequence of water-rock interaction along a flow path** (Chebotarev, 1955). As groundwater matures:

$$HCO_3^- \;\rightarrow\; SO_4^{2-} \;\rightarrow\; Cl^-$$

and on the cation side, progressive cation exchange replaces $Ca^{2+}$ with $Na^+$. The Hill diagram is a **visual fingerprint** of where a water sample lies on this evolutionary sequence.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Hill-Piper procedure consists of **five sequential operations**:

1. **Conversion to meq/L** — Convert all ionic concentrations from mg/L to milliequivalents per litre using:
$$meq/L = \frac{mg/L \times \text{Valence}}{\text{Atomic / Molecular Weight}}$$

2. **Ion Grouping** — Combine $Na^+$ with $K^+$ (treated as a single alkali group) and $HCO_3^-$ with $CO_3^{2-}$ (alkalinity group).

3. **Percentage Calculation** — Express each ion as a **percentage of the total cation sum** and the **total anion sum** separately. The two percentages need *not* sum to 100% across each other — each side sums to 100% within its own group.

4. **Triangular Plotting** — Plot the cation percentage on the lower-left triangle and the anion percentage on the lower-right triangle.

5. **Diamond Projection** — Project straight lines from the cation point and the anion point into the central diamond. The intersection point is the **water's hydrochemical facies identity**.

## 2.2 The Six Hydrochemical Facies (Back, 1961, used with Hill)

| Facies | Cation Dominant | Anion Dominant | Geochemical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** | $Ca^{2+}$ | $HCO_3^-$ | Recharge water, young, shallow, fresh |
| **Type II** | $Na^+$ | $HCO_3^-$ | Ion-exchange water, advanced flow |
| **Type III** | $Na^+$ | $Cl^-$ | Deep, mature, end-point brine |
| **Type IV** | $Ca^{2+}$ | $Cl^-$ | Reverse ion-exchange / mixing zone |
| **Type V** | $Ca^{2+}+Mg^{2+}$ | $Cl^-+SO_4^{2-}$ | Mixed type, transitional |
| **Type VI** | $Na^+$ | $SO_4^{2-}$ | Gypsum/anhydrite dissolution signature |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **must-memorize** compilation for the 14-mark ESE question. The vertical bar symbol `\vert` is used in place of `|` to keep markdown tables valid.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | mg/L → meq/L | $meq/L = \dfrac{mg/L \times \text{valency}}{\text{mol. wt.}}$ | meq/L | Unit conversion step |
| 2 | Cation % | $\%Ca = \dfrac{Ca^{2+}}{\Sigma \text{Cations}} \times 100$ | % | Lower-left triangle |
| 3 | Anion % | $\%Cl = \dfrac{Cl^-}{\Sigma \text{Anions}} \times 100$ | % | Lower-right triangle |
| 4 | Ion Balance Error | $\%IBE = \dfrac{\Sigma C - \Sigma A}{\Sigma C + \Sigma A} \times 100$ | % | Acceptable if $\vert IBE \vert \le 5\%$ |
| 5 | Total Hardness | $TH = 2.5 \times Ca^{2+} + 4.12 \times Mg^{2+}$ (as $CaCO_3$) | mg/L | Hardness classification |
| 6 | SAR | $SAR = \dfrac{Na^+}{\sqrt{(Ca^{2+}+Mg^{2+})/2}}$ | — | Irrigation suitability |
| 7 | TDS (sum method) | $TDS \approx 0.64 \times EC \,(\mu S/cm)$ | mg/L | Salinity check |
| 8 | Residual $Na_2CO_3$ | $RSC = (HCO_3^- + CO_3^{2-}) - (Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+})$ | meq/L | Black alkali hazard |
| 9 | Permeability Index | $PI = \dfrac{Na^+ + \sqrt{HCO_3^-}}{Ca^{2+}+Mg^{2+}+Na^+} \times 100$ | % | Long-term irrigation |
| 10 | Kelly's Ratio | $KR = \dfrac{Na^+}{Ca^{2+}+Mg^{2+}}$ | — | Irrigation: $KR<1$ safe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Weights to Memorize (Board-Examiner Favourite):**
> - $Ca^{2+} = 20.04$,  $Mg^{2+} = 12.15$,  $Na^+ = 23.0$,  $K^+ = 39.1$
> - $HCO_3^- = 61.0$,  $CO_3^{2-} = 30.0$,  $SO_4^{2-} = 48.0$,  $Cl^- = 35.45$

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Aquifer characterisation:** Distinguishing recharge zones from discharge zones.
- **Salinity source identification:** Differentiating anthropogenic contamination from natural brine upconing.
- **Seawater intrusion studies:** Coastal aquifers show a clear **Type I → Type IV → Type III** evolution as one moves inland → coast.
- **Geothermal exploration:** Hot springs plot as Type III/IV due to deep circulation.
- **Drinking water standardisation:** Rapid pre-screening against BIS/WHO limits before detailed lab tests.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical

## 3.1 The Hill-Piper Numerical Procedure — Full Derivation

We will derive, step by step, the **percentage ion computation** that is the foundation of every Hill diagram plot. The following worked example is a **model 14-mark problem**.

### 3.1.1 Given Hydrochemical Analysis (Synthetic Groundwater Sample)

| Ion | $Ca^{2+}$ | $Mg^{2+}$ | $Na^+$ | $K^+$ | $HCO_3^-$ | $SO_4^{2-}$ | $Cl^-$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| mg/L | 80 | 24 | 46 | 3.9 | 244 | 14.4 | 35.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> This dataset is a KTU model question. The unit-conversion step is the single most error-prone calculation in the exam — examiners look for it specifically.

### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Convert mg/L to meq/L

For each ion, $meq/L = \dfrac{mg/L \times \text{valency}}{\text{molecular weight}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ca^{2+}_{meq} &= \frac{80 \times 2}{40.08} = \frac{160}{40.08} = 3.992 \;\approx\; 4.00 \\
Mg^{2+}_{meq} &= \frac{24 \times 2}{24.31} = \frac{48}{24.31} = 1.974 \;\approx\; 1.97 \\
Na^+_{meq} &= \frac{46 \times 1}{23.00} = 2.000 \\
K^+_{meq} &= \frac{3.9 \times 1}{39.10} = 0.0997 \;\approx\; 0.10 \\
HCO_3^-_{meq} &= \frac{244 \times 1}{61.02} = 3.998 \;\approx\; 4.00 \\
SO_4^{2-}_{meq} &= \frac{14.4 \times 2}{96.06} = \frac{28.8}{96.06} = 0.300 \\
Cl^-_{meq} &= \frac{35.5 \times 1}{35.45} = 1.001 \;\approx\; 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> The valency-multiplied numerator is **charge carried per litre**; dividing by molecular weight rescales it to **moles of charge**.

### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Group and Sum Cations and Anions

The Hill-Piper convention groups potassium with sodium, and carbonate with bicarbonate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma \text{Cations} &= Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+} + (Na^+ + K^+) \\
\Sigma \text{Cations} &= 4.00 + 1.97 + 2.00 + 0.10 = 8.07 \;\text{meq/L} \\[6pt]
\Sigma \text{Anions} &= (HCO_3^- + CO_3^{2-}) + SO_4^{2-} + Cl^- \\
\Sigma \text{Anions} &= 4.00 + 0.30 + 1.00 = 5.30 \;\text{meq/L}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Ion Balance Error (Quality Check)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%IBE &= \frac{\Sigma C - \Sigma A}{\Sigma C + \Sigma A} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{8.07 - 5.30}{8.07 + 5.30} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2.77}{13.37} \times 100 = 20.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **An IBE of 20.7% is NOT acceptable** (limit is $\vert IBE \vert \le 5\%$). The dataset above is mathematically constructed to demonstrate the procedure; in a real KTU question, an acceptable dataset will be given. **Always compute and report the IBE first** — it is worth 2 marks by itself.

### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Compute Cation Percentages (for Lower-Left Triangle)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%Ca &= \frac{4.00}{8.07}\times 100 = 49.6\% \\
\%Mg &= \frac{1.97}{8.07}\times 100 = 24.4\% \\
\%(Na+K) &= \frac{2.10}{8.07}\times 100 = 26.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> Check: $49.6 + 24.4 + 26.0 = 100.0\% \;\;\checkmark$

### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Compute Anion Percentages (for Lower-Right Triangle)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%HCO_3 &= \frac{4.00}{5.30}\times 100 = 75.5\% \\
\%SO_4 &= \frac{0.30}{5.30}\times 100 = 5.6\% \\
\%Cl &= \frac{1.00}{5.30}\times 100 = 18.9\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> Check: $75.5 + 5.6 + 18.9 = 100.0\% \;\;\checkmark$

### 3.1.7 Step 6 — Diamond Projection & Facies Identification

The cation point $(49.6, 24.4, 26.0)$ plotted on the cation triangle, and the anion point $(75.5, 5.6, 18.9)$ plotted on the anion triangle, are projected linearly into the central diamond. The intersection lands in the **Ca–HCO₃ sub-region** of the diamond.

$$
\boxed{\text{Water Type} = \text{Calcium–Bicarbonate Facies (Type I)}}
$$

> **Geochemical interpretation:** This water represents a **recharge-zone chemistry**, freshly recharged through carbonate terrain (limestone/dolomite), with short residence time in the aquifer. It is typical of shallow wells in unconfined alluvial aquifers.

### 3.1.8 Step 7 — Derived Salinity Parameters

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Hardness} &= 2.5 \times Ca^{2+}_{mg/L} + 4.12 \times Mg^{2+}_{mg/L} \\
&= 2.5 \times 80 + 4.12 \times 24 \\
&= 200 + 98.88 = 298.88 \;\text{mg/L as } CaCO_3 \\[6pt]
\text{SAR} &= \frac{Na^+_{meq/L}}{\sqrt{\frac{Ca^{2+}_{meq/L} + Mg^{2+}_{meq/L}}{2}}} \\
&= \frac{2.00}{\sqrt{\frac{4.00 + 1.97}{2}}} = \frac{2.00}{\sqrt{2.985}} = \frac{2.00}{1.728} = 1.16 \\[6pt]
\text{Kelly's Ratio} &= \frac{Na^+}{Ca^{2+}+Mg^{2+}} = \frac{2.00}{4.00+1.97} = 0.335
\end{aligned}
$$

- Hardness: 298.88 mg/L $\rightarrow$ **Hard water** (200–300 mg/L range, near-very-hard boundary).
- SAR = 1.16 $\rightarrow$ **Excellent** for irrigation (USSL Class S1).
- KR = 0.335 $\rightarrow$ **Suitable** (KR $<$ 1 is safe).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hill Diagram Interpretation Pipeline

The diagram below maps the end-to-end logical flow of the Hill-Piper interpretation methodology. Each block represents a process node; arrows represent data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Lab Data: mg/L of Major Ions] --> B[Unit Conversion: mg/L to meq/L]
    B --> C[Group Ions: Na+K and HCO3+CO3]
    C --> D[Compute Sum of Cations and Anions]
    D --> E[Compute Ion Balance Error IBE]
    E --> F{IBE within +-5 percent}
    F -- No --> G[Reject / Recheck Data]
    F -- Yes --> H[Compute Cation Percentages]
    H --> I[Compute Anion Percentages]
    I --> J[Plot on Cation Triangle]
    I --> K[Plot on Anion Triangle]
    J --> L[Project into Central Diamond]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Identify Hydrochemical Facies]
    M --> N[Interpret Geochemical Evolution]
    N --> O[Compute SAR, TH, RSC, PI]
    O --> P[Final Water Quality Verdict]
```

## 4.2 Facies Classification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Projected Diamond Point] --> Q1{Cation Dominant}
    Q1 -- Ca or Mg --> Q2{Anion Dominant}
    Q1 -- Na --> Q3{Anion Dominant}
    Q2 -- HCO3 --> F1[Type I: Ca-HCO3 Recharge Water]
    Q2 -- SO4 or Cl --> F5[Type V: Mixed Ca-Mg-Cl-SO4]
    Q3 -- HCO3 --> F2[Type II: Na-HCO3 Ion Exchange]
    Q3 -- Cl --> F3[Type III: Na-Cl Deep Brine]
    Q3 -- SO4 --> F6[Type VI: Na-SO4 Gypsum Zone]
    Q2 -- Cl with high Ca --> F4[Type IV: Ca-Cl Reverse Exchange or Mixing]
```

## 4.3 Equivalent-Weight Reference Card (Schematic Memory Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cations
        CA[Ca2+: 20.04]
        MG[Mg2+: 12.15]
        NA[Na+: 23.00]
        K[K+: 39.10]
    end
    subgraph Anions
        HC[HCO3-: 61.02]
        CO[CO3 2-: 30.00]
        SO[SO4 2-: 48.03]
        CL[Cl-: 35.45]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> These schematic block diagrams function as **functional architecture maps**. In the KTU answer sheet, students should redraw the **Piper Trilinear Diagram** (the two triangles + central diamond) by hand, mark the two percentage points, project them, and label the resulting facies. The decision tree above is your *mnemonic* for remembering the six types.
