# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** Define the Hill-Piper Trilinear Diagram and list the six hydrochemical facies it identifies.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hill-Piper Trilinear Diagram is a graphical method of interpreting hydrochemical analysis data by representing the relative percentages (in meq/L) of major cations ($Ca^{2+}$, $Mg^{2+}$, $Na^+ + K^+$) and major anions ($HCO_3^- + CO_3^{2-}$, $SO_4^{2-}$, $Cl^-$) on two triangular fields whose projections are combined in a central diamond field.

The six hydrochemical facies are:
1. **Type I — $Ca$–$HCO_3$** (Recharge water)
2. **Type II — $Na$–$HCO_3$** (Ion-exchange water)
3. **Type III — $Na$–$Cl$** (Deep / end-point water)
4. **Type IV — $Ca$–$Cl$** (Reverse exchange / mixing)
5. **Type V — Mixed $Ca$–$Mg$–$Cl$–$SO_4$**
6. **Type VI — $Na$–$SO_4$** (Sulphate-bearing deep water)

*(Full marks require naming all six with their geochemical significance.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** Why is it necessary to convert ionic concentrations from mg/L to meq/L before plotting on the Hill diagram? What is the role of the ion balance error?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Unit conversion rationale (2 marks):** The Hill diagram plots the **relative chemical reactivity** of ions, not their mass. Since ions of different valency carry different amounts of charge per milligram, the milliequivalent (meq/L) normalises them to a common chemical basis (charge per litre). A sample with 100 mg/L of $Ca^{2+}$ is chemically very different from 100 mg/L of $Na^+$, and the meq/L conversion captures this difference.

2. **Ion balance error (1 mark):** The IBE, defined as $\dfrac{\Sigma C - \Sigma A}{\Sigma C + \Sigma A} \times 100$, validates the analytical accuracy. A value within $\pm 5\%$ confirms internal consistency of the lab report before plotting.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Every Part-B question offers an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**. The model answer below presents both options with identical mark distribution (7 + 7).

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]*

> A groundwater sample has the following analysis: $Ca^{2+} = 60$ mg/L, $Mg^{2+} = 18$ mg/L, $Na^+ = 92$ mg/L, $K^+ = 3.9$ mg/L, $HCO_3^- = 256$ mg/L, $SO_4^{2-} = 28.8$ mg/L, $Cl^- = 42.6$ mg/L.
>
> **(a)** Convert all ions to meq/L, compute the ion balance error, and plot the data on a Hill-Piper diagram. Identify the hydrochemical facies. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compute Total Hardness, SAR, and Kelly's Ratio. Comment on the irrigation and drinking-water suitability of the sample as per standard classifications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Conversion to meq/L (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ca^{2+}_{meq} &= \frac{60 \times 2}{40.08} = 2.994 \;\approx\; 3.00 \\
Mg^{2+}_{meq} &= \frac{18 \times 2}{24.31} = 1.481 \;\approx\; 1.48 \\
Na^+_{meq} &= \frac{92 \times 1}{23.00} = 4.000 \\
K^+_{meq} &= \frac{3.9 \times 1}{39.10} = 0.0997 \;\approx\; 0.10 \\
HCO_3^-_{meq} &= \frac{256 \times 1}{61.02} = 4.195 \;\approx\; 4.20 \\
SO_4^{2-}_{meq} &= \frac{28.8 \times 2}{96.06} = 0.600 \\
Cl^-_{meq} &= \frac{42.6 \times 1}{35.45} = 1.202 \;\approx\; 1.20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sums and IBE (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma C &= 3.00 + 1.48 + 4.00 + 0.10 = 8.58 \;\text{meq/L} \\
\Sigma A &= 4.20 + 0.60 + 1.20 = 6.00 \;\text{meq/L} \\
\%IBE &= \frac{8.58 - 6.00}{8.58 + 6.00} \times 100 = \frac{2.58}{14.58} \times 100 = 17.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> IBE = 17.7% $\rightarrow$ slightly above the 5% acceptance window. *In an exam, report this and continue with plotting assuming minor analytical error.* *(1 mark deducted if not reported.)*

**Step 3 — Percentages and Facies (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%Ca &= \frac{3.00}{8.58}\times 100 = 35.0\% \\
\%Mg &= \frac{1.48}{8.58}\times 100 = 17.2\% \\
\%(Na+K) &= \frac{4.10}{8.58}\times 100 = 47.8\% \\[4pt]
\%HCO_3 &= \frac{4.20}{6.00}\times 100 = 70.0\% \\
\%SO_4 &= \frac{0.60}{6.00}\times 100 = 10.0\% \\
\%Cl &= \frac{1.20}{6.00}\times 100 = 20.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> Plotting cation point (35, 17.2, 47.8) on the cation triangle and anion point (70, 10, 20) on the anion triangle, and projecting into the diamond, the point falls in the **Type II — Sodium–Bicarbonate Facies**.
>
> **Geochemical Interpretation:** This signature indicates **advanced ion-exchange water** typical of deep, long-residence-time groundwater. Sodium released from clay-mineral exchange has replaced calcium on exchange sites, marking mature flow-path chemistry.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Hardness} &= 2.5 \times 60 + 4.12 \times 18 \\
&= 150 + 74.16 = 224.16 \;\text{mg/L as } CaCO_3 \\[6pt]
SAR &= \frac{Na^+_{meq}}{\sqrt{(Ca^{2+}_{meq} + Mg^{2+}_{meq})/2}} \\
&= \frac{4.00}{\sqrt{(3.00 + 1.48)/2}} = \frac{4.00}{\sqrt{2.24}} = \frac{4.00}{1.497} = 2.67 \\[6pt]
\text{Kelly's Ratio} &= \frac{4.00}{3.00 + 1.48} = \frac{4.00}{4.48} = 0.893
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verdicts:**
- **Hardness (224.16 mg/L):** **Hard water** (BIS limit for drinking: 200 mg/L; slightly above). Treatment by softening advised for domestic supply.
- **SAR (2.67):** **Low sodium hazard** (USSL Class S1). Excellent for irrigation on all soil types.
- **Kelly's Ratio (0.893):** Below 1.0 $\rightarrow$ **Suitable for irrigation** (KR > 1 indicates sodium accumulation risk).
- **Type II Facies:** Suitable for drinking after hardness reduction; excellent for agriculture.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]*

> Given two groundwater samples from a coastal aquifer:
> - **Sample A** (inland, 5 km from coast): $Ca^{2+} = 50$, $Mg^{2+} = 15$, $Na^+ = 30$, $K^+ = 2$, $HCO_3^- = 200$, $SO_4^{2-} = 20$, $Cl^- = 30$ (all in mg/L).
> - **Sample B** (near coast, 0.5 km from coast): $Ca^{2+} = 110$, $Mg^{2+} = 40$, $Na^+ = 240$, $K^+ = 6$, $HCO_3^- = 180$, $SO_4^{2-} = 50$, $Cl^- = 380$ (all in mg/L).
>
> **(a)** Convert both samples to meq/L, plot on a Hill-Piper diagram, and identify the facies of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the hydrochemical evolution from Sample A to Sample B in the context of **seawater intrusion**. Use the **Chloro-alkaline indices** to support your conclusion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Sample A — meq/L conversion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ca^{2+} &= \frac{50\times 2}{40.08} = 2.495 \;\approx\; 2.50 \\
Mg^{2+} &= \frac{15\times 2}{24.31} = 1.234 \;\approx\; 1.23 \\
Na^+ &= \frac{30\times 1}{23.00} = 1.304 \;\approx\; 1.30 \\
K^+ &= \frac{2\times 1}{39.10} = 0.051 \\
HCO_3^- &= \frac{200\times 1}{61.02} = 3.278 \;\approx\; 3.28 \\
SO_4^{2-} &= \frac{20\times 2}{96.06} = 0.416 \;\approx\; 0.42 \\
Cl^- &= \frac{30\times 1}{35.45} = 0.846 \;\approx\; 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma C &= 2.50 + 1.23 + 1.30 + 0.05 = 5.08 \\
\Sigma A &= 3.28 + 0.42 + 0.85 = 4.55 \\
\%IBE &= \frac{5.08 - 4.55}{5.08 + 4.55}\times 100 = 5.5\% \;\;\text{(Acceptable)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sample A — Percentages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%Ca &= 49.2\%, \quad \%Mg = 24.2\%, \quad \%(Na+K) = 26.6\% \\
\%HCO_3 &= 72.1\%, \quad \%SO_4 = 9.2\%, \quad \%Cl = 18.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Facies: Type I — Calcium–Bicarbonate** *(Recharge water; inland, unconfined, short residence time.)*

**Sample B — meq/L conversion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Ca^{2+} &= \frac{110\times 2}{40.08} = 5.489 \;\approx\; 5.49 \\
Mg^{2+} &= \frac{40\times 2}{24.31} = 3.290 \\
Na^+ &= \frac{240\times 1}{23.00} = 10.435 \;\approx\; 10.43 \\
K^+ &= \frac{6\times 1}{39.10} = 0.153 \\
HCO_3^- &= \frac{180\times 1}{61.02} = 2.950 \\
SO_4^{2-} &= \frac{50\times 2}{96.06} = 1.041 \\
Cl^- &= \frac{380\times 1}{35.45} = 10.720
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Sigma C &= 5.49 + 3.29 + 10.43 + 0.15 = 19.36 \\
\Sigma A &= 2.95 + 1.04 + 10.72 = 14.71 \\
\%IBE &= \frac{19.36 - 14.71}{19.36 + 14.71}\times 100 = 13.7\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *IBE of 13.7% is high — in an exam, report it as a "minor imbalance" and proceed.*

**Sample B — Percentages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%Ca &= 28.4\%, \quad \%Mg = 17.0\%, \quad \%(Na+K) = 54.6\% \\
\%HCO_3 &= 20.1\%, \quad \%SO_4 = 7.1\%, \quad \%Cl = 72.8\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Facies: Type III — Sodium–Chloride** *(Deep mature water / strong seawater influence.)*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Hydrochemical Evolution:**

The shift from **Type I (Ca–HCO₃)** in the inland sample to **Type III (Na–Cl)** in the coastal sample is a textbook signature of **seawater intrusion**. Chloride rises from 18.7% to 72.8% of the anion budget; sodium rises from 26.6% to 54.6% of the cation budget. This is geochemically impossible from rock–water interaction alone — it requires mixing with a Na–Cl-rich end member (seawater).

**Chloro-Alkaline Indices (Schoeller, 1965):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CAI_1 &= \frac{Cl^- - (Na^+ + K^+)}{Cl^-} \\
CAI_2 &= \frac{Cl^- - (Na^+ + K^+)}{HCO_3^- + SO_4^{2-} + CO_3^{2-} + NO_3^-}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For Sample A (all values in meq/L):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CAI_1 &= \frac{0.85 - 1.35}{0.85} = \frac{-0.50}{0.85} = -0.588 \\
CAI_2 &= \frac{0.85 - 1.35}{3.28 + 0.42} = \frac{-0.50}{3.70} = -0.135
\end{aligned}
$$

> Both indices **negative** $\rightarrow$ **Normal ion exchange** ($Ca^{2+}/Mg^{2+}$ in water replaced by $Na^+$ from clay).

**For Sample B:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CAI_1 &= \frac{10.72 - 10.58}{10.72} = \frac{0.14}{10.72} = +0.013 \\
CAI_2 &= \frac{10.72 - 10.58}{2.95 + 1.04} = \frac{0.14}{3.99} = +0.035
\end{aligned}
$$

> Both indices **positive** $\rightarrow$ **Reverse ion exchange** (seawater intrusion signature). The sign reversal from negative (Sample A) to positive (Sample B) is **diagnostic** of base-exchange during marine intrusion.

**Conclusion:** The Hill-Piper facies shift and the chloro-alkaline index sign-reversal together **confirm active seawater intrusion** in the coastal well. Recommended remediation: abstraction barrier wells, freshwater injection, or managed aquifer recharge.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Hill-Piper questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the IBE check (–2 marks).** Many students directly jump to plotting. The ion balance error is the **first quality-control step** and carries standalone weight.
> 2. **Wrong equivalent weights (–1 to –3 marks).** Using $Ca = 40$ instead of 40.08, or treating $SO_4$ as monovalent. Memorize the table in §2.3.
> 3. **Plotting the raw percentages (–2 marks).** The Hill diagram uses **percentages relative to total cations** and **percentages relative to total anions**, not absolute values. The two sides each sum to 100% internally — but a sample with 60% Na on the cation side can still have only 30% Na on a *whole-sample* basis.
> 4. **Failing to interpret the facies (–3 marks).** A correct plot without geochemical interpretation is incomplete. Always add: *What does this water type mean in terms of flow path, residence time, and contamination?*
> 5. **Confusion between Piper and Durov diagrams (–1 mark).** Piper has *two triangles + one diamond*. Durov has *two triangles + one rectangle*. The Hill-Piper is the **Piper** variant.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hill-Piper diagram** = two triangular fields (cations, anions) + central diamond for combined facies identification.
- **Always** start with mg/L → meq/L conversion using $E = \dfrac{M}{\text{valency}}$ (or equivalently, $meq/L = mg/L \times \text{valency} / \text{mol.\,wt.}$).
- **Group** $Na$ with $K$, and $HCO_3$ with $CO_3$ — this is a *convention* and must be followed.
- **Compute and report IBE** before plotting; acceptable range is $\vert IBE \vert \le 5\%$.
- **Six facies:** $Ca$–$HCO_3$, $Na$–$HCO_3$, $Na$–$Cl$, $Ca$–$Cl$, mixed $Ca$–$Mg$–$Cl$–$SO_4$, $Na$–$SO_4$.
- **Facies evolution along flow path:** Type I $\rightarrow$ II $\rightarrow$ III (Chebotarev sequence).
- **Seawater intrusion signature:** Facies shift Type I $\rightarrow$ IV $\rightarrow$ III; CAI sign reversal from negative to positive.
- **Hardness classification (mg/L as $CaCO_3$):** Soft $<$ 75; Moderately hard 75–150; Hard 150–300; Very hard $>$ 300.
- **Irrigation thresholds:** SAR $<$ 10 low, 10–18 medium, $>$ 18 high sodium hazard. KR $<$ 1 safe; RSC $<$ 1.25 safe.
- **Exam trick:** If the question gives $K^+$ separately, combine it with $Na^+$ before plotting. If it gives $CO_3^{2-}$ separately, combine with $HCO_3^-$.
- **Equivalents to memorize:** $Ca = 20$, $Mg = 12.15$, $Na = 23$, $K = 39.1$, $HCO_3 = 61$, $CO_3 = 30$, $SO_4 = 48$, $Cl = 35.45$.
- **Geological rule of thumb:** Limestone aquifer $\rightarrow$ $Ca$–$HCO_3$ water; Basalt $\rightarrow$ $Ca$–$Mg$–$HCO_3$; Sandstone with gypsum $\rightarrow$ $Ca$–$SO_4$; Coastal/saline $\rightarrow$ $Na$–$Cl$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piper Trilinear Diagram, Durov's Diagram, and U.S. Salinity Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET835 - HYDROGEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piper Trilinear Diagram, Durov's Diagram, and U.S. Salinity Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Hydrochemical Facies Diagrams

In hydrogeology, raw chemical data from water samples (concentrations of Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺, K⁺, HCO₃⁻, SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻) is often difficult to interpret directly. **Hydrochemical facies diagrams** are graphical tools that visually classify groundwater based on its dissolved ionic composition, helping engineers and geologists understand water quality, origin, and suitability for various uses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrochemical Facies** = A term coined by Morgan and Winner (1962) to denote groundwater having similar chemical characteristics derived from similar geological history. Three principal diagrams are universally used: **Piper Trilinear**, **Durov's**, and **U.S. Salinity Diagram**.

## 1.2 The Piper Trilinear Diagram

### Formal Definition
The **Piper Trilinear Diagram** (1944, Arthur M. Piper) is a graphical representation consisting of two lower equilateral triangles and one upper diamond-shaped field, used to classify water based on the relative proportions of major cations and anions expressed as **percent reacting values** (milliequivalent percentages).

### Conceptual Analogy 🍹
> Imagine you have a glass of mixed juice containing **three** fruit concentrates — orange, apple, and grape. The Piper diagram is like a "flavor triangle" that tells you the percentage of each fruit in your glass. Similarly, in water, we plot the percentage of three major **cations** (left triangle) and three major **anions** (right triangle), and the combined "flavor profile" of your water sample is revealed in the **diamond above**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Cations Plotted:** Ca²⁺ (Calcium), Mg²⁺ (Magnesium), Na⁺+K⁺ (Sodium + Potassium)
> **Three Anions Plotted:** CO₃²⁻ + HCO₃⁻ (Carbonate + Bicarbonate), SO₄²⁻ (Sulfate), Cl⁻ (Chloride)
> Units must always be converted to **meq/L** before plotting.

## 1.3 Durov's Diagram

### Formal Definition
The **Durov Diagram** (Durov, 1948) is composed of two adjacent equilateral triangles (left for cations, right for anions) joined at a common origin, with a central rectangular field. It is used for hydrochemical classification and additionally displays parameters like **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** and **pH** within the central field.

### Conceptual Analogy 🎯
> Think of Durov's diagram as a "two-door entry system" to a central diagnostic chamber. Each ion enters from a triangle (its "door"), and once both cation and anion percentages are plotted, the point lands in the **central rectangle** — which is like a doctor's diagnostic room that not only names the water type but also gives extra health stats (TDS, pH).

## 1.4 U.S. Salinity Diagram

### Formal Definition
The **U.S. Salinity Diagram** (U.S. Salinity Laboratory Staff, 1954) is specifically designed for **irrigation water classification**. It plots the **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** in dS/m or µmhos/cm on the vertical axis (representing **salinity hazard**) against the **Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)** on the horizontal axis (representing **sodium hazard**).

### Conceptual Analogy 🌾
> Picture two "danger meters" facing a farmer. The vertical meter measures **how salty** the water is (high EC = bad for crops), and the horizontal meter measures **how much sodium** the water contains (high sodium = ruins soil structure by dispersing clay). The U.S. Salinity Diagram combines both meters into one chart with **16 zones** of irrigation suitability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard constant in U.S. Salinity Diagram:** SAR is dimensionless and uses concentrations in **meq/L**.
> The diagram classifies water into **C1–C4** (Conductivity/Salinity) and **S1–S4** (Sodium) classes, forming a 4×4 = 16 class matrix.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Piper Trilinear Diagram — Detailed Mechanics

### Step 1: Convert All Ion Concentrations to meq/L

The first step is conversion from mg/L to **milliequivalents per litre (meq/L)** using:

$$ \text{meq/L} = \frac{\text{Concentration in mg/L} \times \text{Valence}}{\text{Atomic/Molecular Weight}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU, the equivalency factors (mg/L → meq/L) most often required are:
> - Ca²⁺: multiply mg/L by **0.0499**
> - Mg²⁺: multiply mg/L by **0.0822**
> - Na⁺: multiply mg/L by **0.0435**
> - K⁺: multiply mg/L by **0.0256**
> - HCO₃⁻: multiply mg/L by **0.0164**
> - SO₄²⁻: multiply mg/L by **0.0208**
> - Cl⁻: multiply mg/L by **0.0282**

### Step 2: Calculate Percent Reacting Values

The percent reacting value of any ion is its **share of the total cation (or anion) charge**:

$$ \% \text{ cation}_i = \frac{\text{meq/L of cation}_i}{\sum \text{meq/L of all cations}} \times 100 $$

$$ \% \text{ anion}_i = \frac{\text{meq/L of anion}_i}{\sum \text{meq/L of all anions}} \times 100 $$

### Step 3: Plot on the Two Triangles
- **Left triangle (cations):** Plot Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, (Na⁺+K⁺)
- **Right triangle (anions):** Plot (CO₃²⁻+HCO₃⁻), SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻

### Step 4: Project Lines into the Diamond
Lines drawn parallel to the **outer edges** of the triangles from each plotted point project upward and intersect in the central **diamond-shaped field**, defining the water type.

## 2.2 Classification of Water Types on Piper Diagram

| Subdivision | Cation Facies | Anion Facies | Water Type | Hydrogeological Significance |
|-------------|---------------|--------------|------------|------------------------------|
| **1** | Ca²⁺ dominant | HCO₃⁻ dominant | Ca-HCO₃ | Recharge water, fresh, temporary hardness |
| **2** | Na⁺ dominant | HCO₃⁻ dominant | Na-HCO₃ | Ion-exchange water, deep circulation |
| **3** | Na⁺ dominant | Cl⁻ dominant | Na-Cl | Saline, end-point of flow path |
| **4** | Ca²⁺ dominant | Cl⁻ dominant | Ca-Cl | Mixed water, contamination |
| **5** | Ca²⁺ dominant | SO₄²⁻ dominant | Ca-SO₄ | Gypsum-bearing formations |
| **6** | Na⁺ dominant | SO₄²⁻ dominant | Na-SO₄ | Mixed/saline |
| **7** | Mg²⁺ dominant | HCO₃⁻ dominant | Mg-HCO₃ | Dolomitic terrain |
| **8** | Mg²⁺ dominant | Cl⁻ dominant | Mg-Cl | Marine/mixed origin |
| **9** | Mg²⁺ dominant | SO₄²⁻ dominant | Mg-SO₄ | Evaporitic origin |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Piper diagrams are extensively used in environmental impact assessments (EIA), landfill leachate studies, and groundwater contamination mapping. In India, they are routinely used by **CGWB (Central Ground Water Board)** for aquifer characterization.

## 2.3 Durov's Diagram — Detailed Mechanics

### Construction
- **Two triangles** share a common point (origin) on their base.
- The **left triangle** is for cations: Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, (Na⁺+K⁺).
- The **right triangle** is for anions: HCO₃⁻+CO₃²⁻, SO₄²⁻, Cl⁻.
- The **central rectangle** receives the projected data from both triangles.
- The rectangle can be subdivided to plot **TDS (mg/L)** and **pH** simultaneously.

### Nine Water-Type Zones in Durov's Central Rectangle
| Zone | Cation Trend | Anion Trend | TDS Range | Interpretation |
|------|--------------|-------------|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | Na increases | Cl increases | High | Saline, advanced evolution |
| 2 | Na increases | SO₄ increases | High | Brine origin |
| 3 | Na increases | HCO₃ increases | Moderate | Recharge with Na-rich |
| 4 | Ca decreases | HCO₃ increases | Low | Fresh recharge |
| 5 | Ca-Mg stable | Cl stable | Variable | Mixing zone |
| 6 | Ca-Mg stable | SO₄ stable | Variable | Gypsum terrain |
| 7 | Ca-Mg stable | HCO₃ stable | Low | Standard water |
| 8 | Ca decreases | Cl increases | High | Reverse ion exchange |
| 9 | Ca decreases | SO₄ increases | High | Marine intrusion |

## 2.4 U.S. Salinity Diagram — Detailed Mechanics

### The Critical Formula: Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\frac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}} $$

where all concentrations are expressed in **meq/L**.

### Classification Scheme
| Class | Salinity (EC in dS/m) | Suitability |
|-------|----------------------|-------------|
| **C1** | < 0.25 | Low salinity — safe for all crops |
| **C2** | 0.25 – 0.75 | Medium salinity — most crops |
| **C3** | 0.75 – 2.25 | High salinity — salt-tolerant crops |
| **C4** | > 2.25 | Very high salinity — only very tolerant crops |

| Class | SAR | Sodium Hazard |
|-------|-----|---------------|
| **S1** | < 10 | Low — safe for all soils |
| **S2** | 10 – 18 | Medium — sodic-sensitive crops at risk |
| **S3** | 18 – 26 | High — gypsum amendments needed |
| **S4** | > 26 | Very high — unsuitable for most soils |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units | Application |
|-------------------|-------------------|-------|-------------|
| mg/L to meq/L | $\text{meq/L} = \dfrac{\text{mg/L} \times \text{Valence}}{\text{Molecular Weight}}$ | meq/L | Universal first step |
| % Reacting Value | $\%_i = \dfrac{C_i}{\sum C} \times 100$ | % | Piper & Durov plotting |
| Total Hardness | $\text{TH} = 2.5[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Water quality |
| Sodium Adsorption Ratio | $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}}$ | dimensionless | U.S. Salinity diagram |
| Residual Sodium Carbonate | $\text{RSC} = (\text{HCO}_3^- + \text{CO}_3^{2-}) - (\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+})$ | meq/L | Irrigation suitability |
| Kelly's Ratio | $\text{KR} = \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]}{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}$ | dimensionless | Irrigation (>1 = unsuitable) |
| Electrical Conductivity–TDS | $\text{TDS} \approx 0.64 \times \text{EC}$ (in µS/cm) | mg/L | U.S. Salinity diagram y-axis |
| Piping Ratio | $\text{PR} = \dfrac{\text{Na}^+ + \sqrt{\text{HCO}_3^-}}{\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{Na}^+}$ | dimensionless | Internal erosion (>20) |

> [!NOTE]
> In markdown tables, the absolute value `|x|` is avoided to prevent syntax breakage. In actual LaTeX, $\vert x \vert$ is the correct notation for the modulus, used inside the SAR denominator.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example (Piper Diagram)

> A groundwater sample has the following analysis:
> - Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L, Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L, Na⁺ = 46 mg/L
> - HCO₃⁻ = 244 mg/L, SO₄²⁻ = 24 mg/L, Cl⁻ = 35.5 mg/L
> - EC = 600 µS/cm, pH = 7.8

**Task:** Plot on the Piper Trilinear Diagram, classify, and compute SAR.

### Step 1: Convert Cations to meq/L

$$ \text{Ca}^{2+}: \frac{80 \times 2}{40} = \frac{160}{40} = 4.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{Mg}^{2+}: \frac{24 \times 2}{24} = \frac{48}{24} = 2.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{Na}^+: \frac{46 \times 1}{23} = \frac{46}{23} = 2.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

**Sum of cations** = 4.00 + 2.00 + 2.00 = **8.00 meq/L**

### Step 2: Convert Anions to meq/L

$$ \text{HCO}_3^-: \frac{244 \times 1}{61} = \frac{244}{61} \approx 4.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{SO}_4^{2-}: \frac{24 \times 2}{96} = \frac{48}{96} = 0.50 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{Cl}^-: \frac{35.5 \times 1}{35.5} = \frac{35.5}{35.5} = 1.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

**Sum of anions** = 4.00 + 0.50 + 1.00 = **5.50 meq/L**

> [!NOTE]
> The slight imbalance (8.00 vs 5.50 meq/L) is acceptable within typical analytical error (±5–10%) because the analysis may not include all minor ions (e.g., NO₃⁻, K⁺).

### Step 3: Calculate Percentages

**Cation percentages:**

$$ \%\text{Ca}^{2+} = \frac{4.00}{8.00} \times 100 = 50.0\% $$

$$ \%\text{Mg}^{2+} = \frac{2.00}{8.00} \times 100 = 25.0\% $$

$$ \%\text{Na}^+ = \frac{2.00}{8.00} \times 100 = 25.0\% $$

**Anion percentages:**

$$ \%\text{HCO}_3^- = \frac{4.00}{5.50} \times 100 = 72.7\% $$

$$ \%\text{SO}_4^{2-} = \frac{0.50}{5.50} \times 100 = 9.1\% $$

$$ \%\text{Cl}^- = \frac{1.00}{5.50} \times 100 = 18.2\% $$

### Step 4: Plotting Coordinates
- **Cation point:** (50% Ca²⁺, 25% Mg²⁺, 25% Na⁺) on left triangle
- **Anion point:** (72.7% HCO₃⁻, 9.1% SO₄²⁻, 18.2% Cl⁻) on right triangle

### Step 5: Classification on Diamond
Projection from both triangles into the upper diamond places the point in the **Ca-Mg-HCO₃ dominant zone** → this is **Subdivision 1: Temporary Hardness Water** (Type I water, fresh recharge).

## 3.2 Step-by-Step SAR Computation (U.S. Salinity Diagram)

Using the same sample:

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}} $$

Substituting values in meq/L:

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{2.00}{\sqrt{\dfrac{4.00 + 2.00}{2}}} $$

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{2.00}{\sqrt{\dfrac{6.00}{2}}} $$

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{2.00}{\sqrt{3.00}} $$

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{2.00}{1.7321} \approx 1.155 $$

### Step 6: U.S. Salinity Diagram Classification

- **EC = 600 µS/cm = 0.60 dS/m** → falls in **C2** (0.25 – 0.75 dS/m) → Medium salinity
- **SAR ≈ 1.16** → falls in **S1** (< 10) → Low sodium hazard
- **Final class: C2-S1** → Suitable for irrigation of most crops with **slight salinity restriction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ion Balance Check:** KTU examiners often award 1 mark for verifying the charge balance error:
> $$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\sum \text{Cations} - \sum \text{Anions}}{\sum \text{Cations} + \sum \text{Anions}} \times 100$$
> For this sample: $\frac{8.00 - 5.50}{8.00 + 5.50} \times 100 = \frac{2.50}{13.50} \times 100 = 18.5\%$
> This is **high**, indicating minor ions (NO₃⁻, K⁺, F⁻) were omitted. A balanced analysis should have an error **< 5%**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing the Three Diagrams

| Parameter | Piper Diagram | Durov's Diagram | U.S. Salinity Diagram |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Year/Author** | 1944, Piper | 1948, Durov | 1954, USSL Staff |
| **Parameters Plotted** | % cations, % anions | % cations, % anions, TDS, pH | EC, SAR |
| **Shape** | Two triangles + diamond | Two triangles + rectangle | One rectangular plot |
| **Application** | Water classification (general) | Classification + quality | Irrigation suitability |
| **Special Feature** | Shows water type evolution | Shows geochemical processes | Shows crop suitability |
| **Input Unit** | meq/L → % | meq/L → %, mg/L, pH | meq/L, dS/m |
| **Output** | 9 subdivisions | 9 fields | 16 classes (4×4) |
| **Limitations** | No TDS info | No sodium hazard direct | No Cl⁻/SO₄²⁻ info |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Piper Trilinear Diagram Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["LEFT TRIANGLE - CATIONS"]
        A["APEX Na PLUS K"]
        B["BOTTOM LEFT Ca"]
        C["BOTTOM RIGHT Mg"]
    end
    subgraph T2["RIGHT TRIANGLE - ANIONS"]
        D["APEX Cl"]
        E["BOTTOM LEFT SO4"]
        F["BOTTOM RIGHT HCO3 PLUS CO3"]
    end
    subgraph D1["UPPER DIAMOND FIELD"]
        G["COMBINED CLASSIFICATION ZONE"]
        H["TYPE I Ca-Mg-HCO3"]
        I["TYPE II Na-HCO3"]
        J["TYPE III Na-Cl"]
        K["TYPE IV Ca-Mg-Cl"]
    end
    A --> G
    B --> G
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H
    G --> I
    G --> J
    G --> K
    style G fill:#FFE5B4
    style H fill:#90EE90

```

```mermaid
    end

        TDS["TDS AXIS mg per L"]
        PH["pH AXIS 0 to 14"]

        Z3["ZONE 3 SALINE END MEMBER"]
        Z4["ZONE 4 MIXING"]
    end
    CA --> CR
    CB --> CR
    CC --> CR
    AA --> CR
    AB --> CR
    AC --> CR
    CR --> Z1

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: U.S. Salinity Diagram — 4x4 Class Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Y["Y AXIS - SALINITY HAZARD EC in dS per m"]
        Y1["C1 LESS THAN 0.25 LOW"]
        Y2["C2 0.25 to 0.75 MEDIUM"]
        Y3["C3 0.75 to 2.25 HIGH"]
        Y4["C4 GREATER THAN 2.25 VERY HIGH"]
    end
    subgraph X["X AXIS - SODIUM HAZARD SAR"]
        X1["S1 LESS THAN 10 LOW"]
        X2["S2 10 to 18 MEDIUM"]
        X3["S3 18 to 26 HIGH"]
        X4["S4 GREATER THAN 26 VERY HIGH"]
    end
    Y1 --> X1
    Y1 --> X2
    Y1 --> X3
    Y1 --> X4
    Y2 --> X1
    Y2 --> X2
    Y2 --> X3
    Y2 --> X4
    Y3 --> X1
    Y3 --> X2
    Y3 --> X3
    Y3 --> X4
    Y4 --> X1
    Y4 --> X2
    Y4 --> X3
    Y4 --> X4
    style Y1 fill:#90EE90
    style Y4 fill:#FF6347
    style X1 fill:#90EE90
    style X4 fill:#FF6347
```

## 4.4 Flow Diagram: Workflow of Hydrochemical Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["SAMPLE COLLECTION"] --> L["LAB ANALYSIS ICP-OES or TITRATION"]
    L --> U["UNITS mg per L"]
    U --> M["CONVERT TO meq per L"]
    M --> P["PERCENT REACTING VALUES"]
    P --> A{"WHICH DIAGRAM"}
    A --> P1["PIPER - GENERAL CLASS"]
    A --> P2["DUROV - CLASS PLUS TDS pH"]
    A --> P3["US SALINITY - IRRIGATION"]
    P1 --> R1["WATER TYPE SUBDIVISION"]
    P2 --> R2["GEOCHEMICAL PROCESS"]
    P3 --> R3["C and S CLASS C2-S1 etc"]
    R1 --> DEC["DECISION MAKING"]
    R2 --> DEC
    R3 --> DEC
    style S fill:#FFD700
    style DEC fill:#90EE90
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Piper Trilinear Diagram. What are the three cations and three anions used for plotting?**

**Model Answer:**

The Piper Trilinear Diagram, developed by A.M. Piper in 1944, is a graphical tool used to classify water based on the relative proportions of major dissolved ions expressed as **percent reacting values** in meq/L.

The three cations plotted are: **Ca²⁺ (Calcium), Mg²⁺ (Magnesium), and Na⁺+K⁺ (Sodium plus Potassium)**.

The three anions plotted are: **CO₃²⁻ + HCO₃⁻ (Carbonate plus Bicarbonate), SO₄²⁻ (Sulfate), and Cl⁻ (Chloride)**.

The diagram consists of two lower equilateral triangles and one upper diamond field, with the points projected from the triangles into the diamond to yield the final water type (Subdivisions 1–9).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Write the formula for Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR). What is its significance in irrigation water classification?**

**Model Answer:**

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}} $$

where all concentrations are in **meq/L**.

**Significance:** SAR is a measure of the **sodium hazard** of irrigation water. High SAR indicates that sodium in water will displace calcium and magnesium from soil particles, causing **clay dispersion, soil structure breakdown, reduced permeability, and hardpan formation**. SAR is plotted on the x-axis of the **U.S. Salinity Diagram** to classify water into S1 (<10, low), S2 (10–18, medium), S3 (18–26, high), and S4 (>26, very high) classes for irrigation suitability.

---

## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (INTERNAL CHOICE)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and use of the **Piper Trilinear Diagram** for classification of groundwater. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A water sample has the following composition in mg/L:
- Ca²⁺ = 60, Mg²⁺ = 18, Na⁺ = 92, K⁺ = 0
- HCO₃⁻ = 305, SO₄²⁻ = 14, Cl⁻ = 35.5

Compute the percent reacting values, classify the water using the Piper diagram, and determine the SAR and U.S. Salinity class. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **Piper Trilinear Diagram** is constructed as follows:

**Components:**
1. **Left lower triangle (cation triangle):** Equilateral triangle with three vertices representing 100% of Ca²⁺ (left), 100% of Mg²⁺ (right), and 100% of Na⁺+K⁺ (top). Each side is graduated from 0 to 100% in 10% intervals.
2. **Right lower triangle (anion triangle):** Equilateral triangle with three vertices representing 100% of HCO₃⁻+CO₃²⁻ (right), 100% of SO₄²⁻ (left), and 100% of Cl⁻ (top).
3. **Upper diamond field:** A rhombus formed by the two triangles, used to display the combined classification.

**Procedure:**
1. Convert all ion concentrations from mg/L to meq/L using equivalent weights.
2. Express each ion as a percentage of the total cation (or anion) charge.
3. Plot the cation point in the left triangle and the anion point in the right triangle.
4. Project lines from the plotted points parallel to the **outer edges** of the triangles into the upper diamond.
5. The intersection of the two projected lines gives the water type.

**Use:** The diagram classifies water into 9 subdivisions (Type I to Type IX), which helps identify recharge zones, ion-exchange processes, salinization, mixing with saline water, and contamination sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sketches in KTU exams:** A clean hand-drawn diagram with labels (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺+K⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, HCO₃⁻) and the diamond is mandatory. Examiners award **1 mark for the sketch** even if the theory is incomplete.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Convert to meq/L**

$$ \text{Ca}^{2+}: \frac{60 \times 2}{40} = 3.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{Mg}^{2+}: \frac{18 \times 2}{24} = 1.50 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{Na}^+: \frac{92 \times 1}{23} = 4.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{K}^+: 0 \text{ meq/L} $$

**Σ Cations = 3.00 + 1.50 + 4.00 = 8.50 meq/L**

$$ \text{HCO}_3^-: \frac{305}{61} = 5.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{SO}_4^{2-}: \frac{14 \times 2}{96} = 0.29 \text{ meq/L} $$

$$ \text{Cl}^-: \frac{35.5}{35.5} = 1.00 \text{ meq/L} $$

**Σ Anions = 5.00 + 0.29 + 1.00 = 6.29 meq/L**

**Step 2: Percentages**

$$ \%\text{Ca}^{2+} = \frac{3.00}{8.50} \times 100 = 35.3\% $$

$$ \%\text{Mg}^{2+} = \frac{1.50}{8.50} \times 100 = 17.6\% $$

$$ \%\text{Na}^+ = \frac{4.00}{8.50} \times 100 = 47.1\% $$

$$ \%\text{HCO}_3^- = \frac{5.00}{6.29} \times 100 = 79.5\% $$

$$ \%\text{SO}_4^{2-} = \frac{0.29}{6.29} \times 100 = 4.6\% $$

$$ \%\text{Cl}^- = \frac{1.00}{6.29} \times 100 = 15.9\% $$

**Step 3: Piper Classification**

Plotting the cation point (35.3% Ca, 17.6% Mg, 47.1% Na) and anion point (79.5% HCO₃, 4.6% SO₄, 15.9% Cl) and projecting into the diamond, the water falls in the **Na-HCO₃ dominant zone** → **Subdivision 2: Sodium-Bicarbonate type** (recharge with possible ion exchange).

**Step 4: SAR Calculation**

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{4.00}{\sqrt{\dfrac{3.00 + 1.50}{2}}} = \frac{4.00}{\sqrt{2.25}} = \frac{4.00}{1.50} = 2.67 $$

**Step 5: U.S. Salinity Class**

Assume EC = 800 µS/cm = 0.80 dS/m.
- EC = 0.80 dS/m → **C3** (0.75–2.25 dS/m) → High salinity
- SAR = 2.67 → **S1** (< 10) → Low sodium hazard
- **Final class: C3-S1** → Suitable for salt-tolerant crops with **good drainage**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often **forget to convert mg/L to meq/L** before plotting on Piper. This results in **wrong classifications** and loss of up to **4 marks**. Always perform the meq/L conversion **first** — this is the single most important step.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Describe the **U.S. Salinity Diagram** in detail. What is the role of **EC** and **SAR** in classifying irrigation water? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A groundwater sample has the following analysis (in meq/L): Ca²⁺ = 3.5, Mg²⁺ = 1.5, Na⁺ = 5.0, HCO₃⁻ = 6.0, SO₄²⁻ = 2.0, Cl⁻ = 2.0. The EC is 950 µS/cm. Classify the water as per the U.S. Salinity Diagram. Also compute the **Residual Sodium Carbonate (RSC)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a)

The **U.S. Salinity Diagram** (Richards, 1954) is a rectangular chart designed specifically for **classifying irrigation water quality**. It has two axes:

**Vertical axis (Y-axis) — Salinity Hazard:**
This axis is the **Electrical Conductivity (EC)** expressed in **dS/m** (deciSiemens per metre) at 25°C. The axis is divided into four zones:
- **C1: Low salinity** (EC < 0.25 dS/m)
- **C2: Medium salinity** (EC 0.25 – 0.75 dS/m)
- **C3: High salinity** (EC 0.75 – 2.25 dS/m)
- **C4: Very high salinity** (EC > 2.25 dS/m)

**Horizontal axis (X-axis) — Sodium Hazard:**
This axis is the **Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)**, a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the relative proportion of sodium to calcium and magnesium.

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}} $$

The axis is divided into four zones:
- **S1: Low sodium** (SAR < 10)
- **S2: Medium sodium** (SAR 10 – 18)
- **S3: High sodium** (SAR 18 – 26)
- **S4: Very high sodium** (SAR > 26)

**Resulting 16 Classes:** The intersection of C and S classes (e.g., C2-S1, C3-S4) gives 16 possible water-quality classes. For example:
- **C1-S1:** Excellent for all crops
- **C4-S4:** Unsuitable for irrigation
- **C2-S2:** Marginal; gypsum amendment may be needed

**Role of EC:** EC reflects the **total salinity** of water, which directly affects crop yield. High EC causes **osmotic stress** in plants, reducing water uptake.

**Role of SAR:** SAR indicates the **sodium hazard**, which causes **soil sodicity** — the replacement of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ in soil by Na⁺, leading to clay dispersion, poor drainage, and crust formation.

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram does NOT directly show **chloride**, **sulfate**, or **bicarbonate** hazards. For complete evaluation, the diagram is used alongside **Wilcox's diagram** and **bicarbonate hazard** assessment.

---

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given (in meq/L):**
- Ca²⁺ = 3.5, Mg²⁺ = 1.5, Na⁺ = 5.0
- HCO₃⁻ = 6.0, SO₄²⁻ = 2.0, Cl⁻ = 2.0
- EC = 950 µS/cm = 0.95 dS/m

**Step 1: Ion Balance Check**

Σ Cations = 3.5 + 1.5 + 5.0 = 10.0 meq/L
Σ Anions = 6.0 + 2.0 + 2.0 = 10.0 meq/L
**Error = 0% — perfectly balanced.**

**Step 2: SAR Calculation**

$$ \text{SAR} = \frac{5.0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{3.5 + 1.5}{2}}} = \frac{5.0}{\sqrt{2.5}} = \frac{5.0}{1.5811} \approx 3.16 $$

**Step 3: U.S. Salinity Classification**

- EC = 0.95 dS/m → falls in **C3** (0.75 – 2.25 dS/m) → **High salinity**
- SAR = 3.16 → falls in **S1** (< 10) → **Low sodium hazard**
- **Class: C3-S1** — High salinity, low sodium.

**Interpretation:** Water is **suitable for irrigation of salt-tolerant crops** like barley, sugar beet, and cotton, but with **adequate drainage** to prevent salt accumulation. Sensitive crops (rice, maize) may yield poorly.

**Step 4: Residual Sodium Carbonate (RSC)**

$$ \text{RSC} = (\text{HCO}_3^- + \text{CO}_3^{2-}) - (\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}) $$

$$ \text{RSC} = (6.0 + 0) - (3.5 + 1.5) = 6.0 - 5.0 = 1.0 \text{ meq/L} $$

**Interpretation of RSC:**
- RSC < 1.25 meq/L → **Safe** for irrigation
- RSC = 1.25 – 2.5 meq/L → **Marginal**
- RSC > 2.5 meq/L → **Unsuitable** (causes black alkali soil)

Hence, with RSC = 1.0 meq/L, this water is **safe for irrigation** in terms of bicarbonate hazard.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** A common error is computing RSC using **mg/L values** instead of **meq/L**. Always verify the unit before substitution. Examiners specifically check this — incorrect unit = **2 mark penalty**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Hydrochemical Diagrams):**

- ✅ **Piper Diagram (1944):** Two triangles + diamond; plots **% reacting values** in meq/L. Nine subdivisions: I = Ca-Mg-HCO₃, II = Na-HCO₃, III = Na-Cl, IV = Ca-Mg-Cl, V = Ca-Mg-SO₄, etc.
- ✅ **Durov Diagram (1948):** Two triangles + **central rectangle** showing **TDS and pH**. Reveals geochemical processes like **ion exchange** and **reverse ion exchange**.
- ✅ **U.S. Salinity Diagram (1954):** Plots **EC (dS/m) vs. SAR**; gives 16 classes (C1–C4, S1–S4). Used for **irrigation water quality** assessment.
- ✅ **Always convert mg/L to meq/L first** using: $\text{meq/L} = \dfrac{\text{mg/L} \times \text{Valence}}{\text{Molecular Weight}}$
- ✅ **% Reacting Value formula:** $\%_i = \dfrac{C_i}{\sum C} \times 100$
- ✅ **SAR formula:** $\text{SAR} = \dfrac{[\text{Na}^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + [\text{Mg}^{2+}]}{2}}}$ — uses **meq/L**.
- ✅ **Ion balance error** should be **< 5%** for a valid analysis.
- ✅ **C1-S1** is the **best** water for irrigation; **C4-S4** is the **worst**.
- ✅ **RSC > 2.5 meq/L** → water is **unsuitable** for irrigation (black alkali).
- ✅ **Salinity Hazard (C)** = osmotic stress on plants; **Sodium Hazard (S)** = soil structural damage.
- ✅ **Piper → Classification**, **Durov → Process + Quality**, **US Salinity → Irrigation Use** — choose the right diagram for the right purpose.
- ✅ **KC (Kelly's Criterion):** KR > 1 → unsuitable for irrigation.
- ✅ **Equivalency factor for Ca²⁺ = 0.0499**, **Mg²⁺ = 0.0822**, **Na⁺ = 0.0435**, **HCO₃⁻ = 0.0164** — memorize these.
- ✅ The **diamond of the Piper diagram** has 6 sub-regions corresponding to the major water types.
- ✅ **Durov's central rectangle** can show **pH (x-axis) and TDS (y-axis)** — useful for hydrochemical evolution studies.
- ✅ **CGWB (India)** uses these diagrams for national aquifer mapping under **NAQUIM**.
- ✅ **KTU Valuer Tip:** Always state the **equation used**, the **unit**, and the **final class**. A 3-step solution (convert → calculate → classify) scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR), WHO, BIS, and ISI Water Quality Standards, Biological Health of Groundwater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET835 - HYDROGEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR), WHO, BIS, and ISI Water Quality Standards, Biological Health of Groundwater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> The **Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)** is an irrigation water quality parameter that quantifies the relative proportion of sodium (Na⁺) to calcium (Ca²⁺) and magnesium (Mg²⁺) ions in water. It is a dimensionless index used to predict the **sodium hazard** of irrigation water and the consequent risk of **soil sodicity**, structural degradation, and reduced infiltration capacity.

Mathematically, SAR is formally defined as:

$$SAR = \frac{\left[Na^+\right]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{\left[Ca^{2+}\right] + \left[Mg^{2+}\right]}{2}}}$$

where all ionic concentrations are expressed in **milliequivalents per litre (meq/L)**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**

Imagine a classroom of children where the teacher (calcium and magnesium) keeps everyone disciplined and seated (good soil structure). Now, suppose a mischievous group of students (sodium) keeps entering the class. When sodium dominates, the "discipline" breaks down and the children start running around chaotically — analogous to how sodium causes clay particles in soil to **disperse**, swell, and lose structure. **SAR tells us exactly how dominant that mischievous sodium crowd is, relative to the disciplinary teachers.** A higher SAR = more chaos in the soil.

> [!NOTE] **Key Insight for KTU Exams**
> - SAR is a **dimensionless ratio**, not a concentration.
> - SAR primarily controls the **Exchangeable Sodium Percentage (ESP)** of soils.
> - The standard empirical relationship is: **ESP ≈ 1.184 × SAR − 1.82** (approximate, valid up to SAR ≈ 40).

---

### 1.2 WHO, BIS, and ISI Drinking Water Standards

**World Health Organization (WHO)** publishes the *Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality* — a globally accepted benchmark for safe potable water. It defines **health-based** guideline values for chemical and microbial constituents.

**Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** is India's national standards body. The relevant Indian Standard is **IS 10500:2012** ("Drinking Water — Specification"), which is the statutory Indian standard. The standard uses the abbreviation **IS** in conjunction with the numerical designation.

> [!IMPORTANT] **ISI = BIS (Statutory Synonym)**
> Historically, the **Indian Standards Institution (ISI)** was India's national body before being renamed **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** in 1987. For all practical and examination purposes, **ISI and BIS are the same body**, and the current standard is **IS 10500:2012**. Any reference to "ISI standard" in a KTU exam is identical to the BIS standard.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of WHO standards as the **international flight safety rules** (very conservative, designed for worldwide applicability), while BIS/ISI standards are the **Indian Railway safety rules** (tailored to local conditions, local population physiology, climate, and contaminant load). For a KTU exam, **BIS (IS 10500:2012) is the binding statutory standard**, while WHO serves as the reference benchmark.

---

### 1.3 Biological Health of Groundwater

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition**
> **Biological health of groundwater** refers to the microbiological quality of subsurface water with respect to the presence or absence of pathogenic (disease-causing) and indicator microorganisms. Groundwater is generally considered the **safest source of drinking water microbiologically** because of natural filtration through aquifers, but is **not immune** to bacterial and viral contamination from septic tanks, landfills, agricultural runoff, and karst conduits.

The most critical biological indicators monitored are:
- **Total Coliforms (TC)**
- **Fecal Coliforms (FC)**
- **Escherichia coli (E. coli)** — the definitive indicator of recent fecal contamination
- **Fecal Streptococci / Enterococci**

> [!NOTE] **Why E. coli?**
> E. coli is naturally present in the intestines of warm-blooded animals and is shed in feces. Its detection in water is **definitive proof of recent fecal contamination** and indicates the likely presence of enteric pathogens such as *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, *Vibrio cholerae*, *Rotavirus*, and parasitic protozoans like *Giardia* and *Cryptosporidium*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Theoretical Basis of SAR

The SAR concept originates from **soil chemistry** and the **Cation Exchange Capacity (CEC)** of clay soils. Clay particles carry a net **negative surface charge**, which attracts and holds positively charged cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Na⁺, K⁺). The relative proportion of these cations determines:

1. **Soil structural stability** — Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ promote **flocculation** (good structure).
2. **Soil dispersivity** — Na⁺ promotes **dispersion** (poor structure, swelling clays).
3. **Hydraulic conductivity** — High Na⁺ reduces infiltration and aeration.

The **Sodium Hazard** is classified as follows (US Salinity Laboratory / FAO classification, universally accepted and KTU-aligned):

| SAR Value | Sodium Hazard Class | Soil Impact |
|-----------|--------------------|-------------|
| < 10 | **Low (S1)** | No restriction; safe for most soils |
| 10 – 18 | **Medium (S2)** | Slight to moderate restriction; problematic for fine-textured soils |
| 18 – 26 | **High (S3)** | Severe restriction; may cause sodicity |
| > 26 | **Very High (S4)** | Unsuitable for irrigation unless gypsum-amended |

> [!NOTE] **Residual Sodium Carbonate (RSC) — Companion Parameter**
> KTU exams often pair SAR with **RSC**, defined as: **RSC = (CO₃²⁻ + HCO₃⁻) − (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺)** in meq/L. RSC > 2.5 meq/L is considered unsafe for irrigation because carbonate/bicarbonate ions will precipitate Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺, leaving Na⁺ to dominate the exchange complex — effectively worsening the SAR hazard.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Standard | Expression | Units | Purpose |
|---|--------------------|-----------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Sodium Adsorption Ratio | $SAR = \dfrac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]}{2}}}$ | dimensionless | Sodium hazard in irrigation water |
| 2 | Exchangeable Sodium Percentage (approximation) | $ESP \approx 1.184 \cdot SAR - 1.82$ | % | Soil sodicity indicator |
| 3 | Residual Sodium Carbonate | $RSC = (CO_3^{2-} + HCO_3^-) - (Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+})$ | meq/L | Carbonate-induced sodicity |
| 4 | Total Hardness | $TH = 2.5[Ca^{2+}] + 4.12[Mg^{2+}]$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Calcium + Magnesium content |
| 5 | Permeability Index | $PI = \dfrac{[Na^+] + \sqrt{[HCO_3^-]}}{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}] + [Na^+]} \times 100$ | % | Long-term soil permeability |
| 6 | Soluble Sodium Percentage | $SSP = \dfrac{[Na^+]}{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}] + [Na^+] + [K^+]} \times 100$ | % | Sodium fraction of total cations |
| 7 | Kelly's Ratio | $KR = \dfrac{[Na^+]}{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]}$ | dimensionless | Irrigation suitability (KR < 1 = suitable) |
| 8 | Magnesium Hazard | $MH = \dfrac{[Mg^{2+}]}{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]} \times 100$ | % | Mg-related soil alkalinity (>50 = hazardous) |
| 9 | Most Probable Number (MPN) | Statistical tabulation from positive/negative tubes | MPN index/100 mL | Coliform enumeration |
| 10 | Membrane Filtration Coliform Count | $CFU/100\,mL$ | colony-forming units | Direct plate count |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU**
> - All ionic concentrations in SAR formula **must be in meq/L**, NOT mg/L.
> - Conversion: meq/L = (mg/L × valency) / atomic weight.
> - For Na⁺: meq/L = mg/L ÷ 23.0
> - For Ca²⁺: meq/L = mg/L ÷ 20.04
> - For Mg²⁺: meq/L = mg/L ÷ 12.15

### 2.3 Water Quality Standards — WHO vs BIS (IS 10500:2012)

| Parameter | WHO Guideline (4th ed.) | BIS IS 10500:2012 (Desirable) | BIS IS 10500:2012 (Max Permissible) | Health Significance |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------|
| **pH** | 6.5 – 8.5 | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation | Acid-base balance, pipe corrosion |
| **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** | 600 mg/L (taste), 1000 mg/L (max) | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L | Salinity, taste, scaling |
| **Total Hardness (as CaCO₃)** | 500 mg/L | 200 mg/L | 600 mg/L | Cardiovascular effects, scaling |
| **Chloride (Cl⁻)** | 250 mg/L | 250 mg/L | 1000 mg/L | Salinity, taste |
| **Sulphate (SO₄²⁻)** | 250 mg/L | 200 mg/L | 400 mg/L | Laxative effects, scaling |
| **Nitrate (as NO₃⁻)** | 50 mg/L (acute, infants) | 45 mg/L | 100 mg/L (NO₃) | Methemoglobinemia ("blue baby") |
| **Fluoride (F⁻)** | 1.5 mg/L | 1.0 mg/L | 1.5 mg/L | Dental/skeletal fluorosis |
| **Iron (Fe)** | 0.3 mg/L (taste) | 0.3 mg/L | No relaxation | Taste, staining, bacterial growth |
| **Arsenic (As)** | 0.01 mg/L (10 µg/L) | 0.01 mg/L | No relaxation (50 µg/L old) | Carcinogenic |
| **Lead (Pb)** | 0.01 mg/L | 0.01 mg/L | No relaxation | Neurotoxic |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | 0.003 mg/L | 0.003 mg/L | No relaxation | Renal toxicity |
| **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺)** | 0.05 mg/L | 0.05 mg/L | No relaxation | Carcinogenic |
| **E. coli / Fecal Coliforms** | 0 / 100 mL | 0 / 100 mL | 0 / 100 mL (no relaxation) | Indicator of fecal contamination |
| **Total Coliforms** | Not detectable in any 100 mL sample | 0 / 100 mL | 0 / 100 mL (MCL absent in 95% samples) | General microbial quality |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner Pitfall**
> Many students incorrectly remember **Arsenic limit as 0.05 mg/L** — this is the **WHO 1993** value. The **current WHO and BIS limit is 0.01 mg/L (10 µg/L)** since 1993 onwards. Always write the **current** value.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agricultural Engineers** use SAR to recommend gypsum (CaSO₄·2H₂O) amendments in sodic soils. The reaction is:
$$Na_2\text{-clay} + CaSO_4 \rightarrow Ca\text{-clay} + Na_2SO_4$$
- **Public Health Engineers** apply BIS limits when designing water treatment plants and recommending community water sources.
- **Hydrogeologists** use microbial limits to delineate well-head protection zones around drinking water wells.
- **Environmental Engineers** use these standards in EIA studies and groundwater remediation projects.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation of SAR from Cation Exchange Equilibrium

The **Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)** is derived from the principle of **cation exchange equilibrium** between irrigation water and soil colloids. The exchange reaction is reversible and follows the **Gapon Equation**:

$$\frac{[Na^+]_{exchangeable}}{[(Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+})]_{exchangeable}} = K_G \cdot \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{[Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+}]}} \cdot \frac{1}{[TEC]^{1/2}}$$

where $K_G$ is the Gapon selectivity constant and TEC is the Total Exchange Capacity. In irrigation classification work, the simplified empirical index used is:

$$SAR = \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]}{2}}}$$

The factor **½** inside the square root arises because Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ are **divalent** (charge = 2), and their effective concentration in the exchange equation must be normalized. The derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1: Start with the Gapon equation applied to sodium vs. calcium-magnesium exchange.**

$$\frac{Na_X}{(Ca + Mg)_X} = K_G \cdot \frac{[Na^+]_{aq}}{\sqrt{[Ca^{2+}]_{aq} + [Mg^{2+}]_{aq}}}$$

**Step 2: Define ESP (Exchangeable Sodium Percentage).**

$$ESP = \frac{Na_X}{CEC} \times 100$$

**Step 3: For practical irrigation classification, the Gapon constant and CEC are lumped into an empirical proportionality, yielding the standard SAR formula.**

$$SAR = \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]}{2}}}$$

**Step 4: Empirical ESP-SAR correlation (US Salinity Laboratory, 1954):**

$$ESP = 1.184 \cdot SAR - 1.82$$

This correlation was established from regression analysis of hundreds of arid-zone soil samples and is valid for SAR values up to approximately **40**. For SAR > 40, the linear relationship deviates.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: SAR Calculation from Ionic Concentrations (in mg/L)

> [!NOTE] **Worked Numerical Problem**
> A groundwater sample has the following ionic analysis (in mg/L):
> - Na⁺ = 230 mg/L
> - Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L
> - Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L
> - HCO₃⁻ = 250 mg/L
> - Cl⁻ = 142 mg/L
>
> Calculate: (a) SAR, (b) ESP, (c) classify sodium hazard, (d) RSC.

**Step 1: Convert all cations from mg/L to meq/L.**

For Na⁺ (atomic weight = 23.0, valency = 1):

$$[Na^+]_{meq/L} = \frac{230 \times 1}{23.0} = 10.00 \text{ meq/L}$$

For Ca²⁺ (atomic weight = 40.08, valency = 2):

$$[Ca^{2+}]_{meq/L} = \frac{80 \times 2}{40.08} = 3.99 \text{ meq/L}$$

For Mg²⁺ (atomic weight = 24.30, valency = 2):

$$[Mg^{2+}]_{meq/L} = \frac{24 \times 2}{24.30} = 1.975 \text{ meq/L}$$

For HCO₃⁻ (atomic weight = 61.0, valency = 1):

$$[HCO_3^-]_{meq/L} = \frac{250 \times 1}{61.0} = 4.098 \text{ meq/L}$$

For Cl⁻ (atomic weight = 35.45, valency = 1):

$$[Cl^-]_{meq/L} = \frac{142 \times 1}{35.45} = 4.006 \text{ meq/L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute into SAR formula.**

$$SAR = \frac{10.00}{\sqrt{\dfrac{3.99 + 1.975}{2}}}$$

$$SAR = \frac{10.00}{\sqrt{\dfrac{5.965}{2}}}$$

$$SAR = \frac{10.00}{\sqrt{2.9825}}$$

$$SAR = \frac{10.00}{1.7270}$$

$$\boxed{SAR = 5.79}$$

**Step 3: Calculate ESP.**

$$ESP = 1.184 \times 5.79 - 1.82 = 6.855 - 1.82 = 5.04\%$$

$$\boxed{ESP = 5.04\%}$$

**Step 4: Classify Sodium Hazard.**

Since SAR = 5.79 < 10, the water falls in the **Low Sodium Hazard (S1)** class. **No irrigation restriction** based on sodium alone.

**Step 5: Calculate RSC.**

$$RSC = (CO_3^{2-} + HCO_3^-) - (Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+})$$

Assuming no carbonate (CO₃²⁻ ≈ 0):

$$RSC = (0 + 4.098) - (3.99 + 1.975) = 4.098 - 5.965 = -1.867 \text{ meq/L}$$

Since RSC is negative, **the water is safe** from carbonate-induced sodicity (RSC < 1.25 = safe; 1.25–2.5 = marginal; > 2.5 = unsafe).

---

### 3.3 Conversion Logic — meq/L vs mg/L (Master Reference Table)

| Ion | Symbol | Atomic / Molecular Weight | Valency | Multiplier: (mg/L → meq/L) |
|-----|--------|--------------------------|---------|---------------------------|
| Sodium | Na⁺ | 23.00 | 1 | ÷ 23.00 |
| Potassium | K⁺ | 39.10 | 1 | ÷ 39.10 |
| Calcium | Ca²⁺ | 40.08 | 2 | ÷ 20.04 |
| Magnesium | Mg²⁺ | 24.30 | 2 | ÷ 12.15 |
| Bicarbonate | HCO₃⁻ | 61.00 | 1 | ÷ 61.00 |
| Carbonate | CO₃²⁻ | 60.00 | 2 | ÷ 30.00 |
| Chloride | Cl⁻ | 35.45 | 1 | ÷ 35.45 |
| Sulphate | SO₄²⁻ | 96.06 | 2 | ÷ 48.03 |
| Nitrate | NO₃⁻ | 62.00 | 1 | ÷ 62.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Universal Conversion Formula:**
> $$\text{meq/L} = \frac{\text{mg/L} \times \text{Valency}}{\text{Atomic or Molecular Weight}}$$

---

### 3.4 Biological Health — Most Probable Number (MPN) Method Logic

The MPN method is a **statistical estimation technique** for coliform enumeration, based on the **Poisson distribution**. The procedure:

1. Inoculate **5 tubes × 3 dilutions** (e.g., 10 mL, 1 mL, 0.1 mL) into lactose broth.
2. Incubate at **35 °C ± 0.5 °C for 24–48 hours**.
3. Count positive tubes (gas production / color change).
4. Read MPN/100 mL from the **standard MPN table** (e.g., 5-5-5 = >1600; 0-0-0 = <2).
5. Confirm with **brilliant green lactose bile (BGLB) broth** for fecal coliforms at **44.5 °C**.

> [!TIP] **KTU Quick Recall**
> - MPN is **statistical, not exact** — confidence limits are wide (typically 95% within ±1 log).
> - **Membrane Filtration (MF)** is more precise but limited to low-turbidity water.
> - **Pour plate / Spread plate** methods give **direct CFU/100 mL** counts but require longer incubation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — SAR Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Water Sample] --> B[Laboratory Ionic Analysis in mg/L]
    B --> C[Convert Cations to meq/L using atomic weight and valency]
    C --> D[Identify Na, Ca, Mg Concentrations in meq/L]
    D --> E{Apply SAR Formula}
    E --> F["SAR equals Na divided by sqrt of Ca plus Mg over 2"]
    F --> G[SAR Numerical Value]
    G --> H{SAR Threshold Classification}
    H -->|SAR less than 10| I1[Class S1 - Low Sodium Hazard]
    H -->|SAR 10 to 18| I2[Class S2 - Medium Sodium Hazard]
    H -->|SAR 18 to 26| I3[Class S3 - High Sodium Hazard]
    H -->|SAR greater than 26| I4[Class S4 - Very High Sodium Hazard]
    I1 --> J[Decision: Safe for Irrigation]
    I2 --> K[Decision: Conditional Use with Gypsum]
    I3 --> L[Decision: Restricted Use, Soil Amendment Required]
    I4 --> M[Decision: Unsuitable, Treat or Blend]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Drinking Water Quality Standard Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Water Source Selection] --> Q{Identify Applicable Standard}
    Q -->|Global Reference| R[WHO Guidelines 4th Edition 2017]
    Q -->|Statutory Indian Standard| S[BIS IS 10500 of 2012]
    Q -->|Legacy Reference| T[ISI Mark Equivalent to BIS]
    R --> U[Compare Parameter against WHO Guideline Value]
    S --> V[Compare Parameter against BIS Desirable Limit]
    V --> W{Exceeds Desirable Limit?}
    W -->|No| X[Compliant - No Treatment Upgrade]
    W -->|Yes| Y[Compare against BIS Permissible Limit]
    Y --> Z{Within Permissible Limit?}
    Z -->|Yes| AA[Conditional Compliance - Monitor and Improve]
    Z -->|No| AB[Non-Compliant - Mandatory Treatment Required]
    U --> AC{Exceeds WHO Guideline?}
    AC -->|No| AD[Globally Compliant]
    AC -->|Yes| AE[Health Risk Identified]
```

### 4.3 Block Architecture — Biological Health Assessment Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Groundwater Sample Collection Sterile Bottle] --> S2[Transport on Ice within 6 Hours]
    S2 --> S3{Selection of Detection Method}
    S3 -->|Quantitative| S4[Multiple Tube Fermentation MPN Method]
    S3 -->|Quantitative| S5[Membrane Filtration MF Method]
    S3 -->|Qualitative Rapid| S6[Defined Substrate Technology like Colilert]
    S4 --> S7[Inoculate Lauryl Tryptose Broth 35 C for 24 to 48 Hours]
    S7 --> S8{Positive Tubes Detected?}
    S8 -->|Yes| S9[Confirm in Brilliant Green Lactose Bile 44.5 C]
    S8 -->|No| S10[Report as Negative for Coliforms]
    S9 --> S11{Confirmed Fecal Coliforms?}
    S11 -->|Yes| S12[Imminent Health Risk - Disinfection Mandatory]
    S11 -->|No| S13[Possible Non-Fecal Coliforms - Investigate Source]
    S5 --> S14[Filter through 0.45 Micron Membrane]
    S14 --> S15[Culture on mFC Agar at 44.5 C]
    S15 --> S16[Count Blue Colonies as CFU per 100 mL]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Block Map — WHO vs BIS vs ISI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GLOBAL
        WHO[WHO Guidelines 2017 - 4th Edition - Health Based - No Enforcement]
    end
    subgraph NATIONAL
        BIS[BIS IS 10500 of 2012 - Statutory - Two Tier - Desirable and Permissible]
    end
    subgraph LEGACY
        ISI[ISI - Pre 1987 Body - Now Renamed BIS - Same Standard Family]
    end
    WHO --> R1[Reference Benchmark]
    BIS --> R2[Legally Enforceable in India]
    ISI --> R3[Historically Equivalent]
    R1 --> O[Operational Compliance Check]
    R2 --> O
    R3 --> O
```
