<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR). State its significance in groundwater studies and the empirical relationship between SAR and Exchangeable Sodium Percentage (ESP).*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** SAR is a dimensionless index that expresses the relative concentration of sodium ions to calcium and magnesium ions in irrigation water, defined mathematically as:
>
> $$SAR = \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]}{2}}}$$
>
> where concentrations are in meq/L.
>
> **Significance:**
> - Predicts the **sodium hazard** of irrigation water.
> - Indicates the risk of **soil sodicity** and consequent structural breakdown (clay dispersion).
> - Used to classify water for irrigation suitability (S1 to S4 classes).
> - Guides gypsum / CaCl₂ amendment requirements.
>
> **Empirical ESP–SAR Relationship:**
> $$ESP = 1.184 \times SAR - 1.82$$
>
> This linear correlation (US Salinity Laboratory, 1954) is valid for SAR values up to approximately 40.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Briefly explain the BIS (IS 10500:2012) drinking water standard. List any five parameters with their desirable and maximum permissible limits.*

**Model Answer:**

> **BIS IS 10500:2012** is the Indian Standard specification for drinking water, published by the Bureau of Indian Standards. It supersedes earlier ISI standards and prescribes **two-tier limits**: (i) **Desirable Limit** (aesthetic acceptance, no health risk) and (ii) **Permissible Limit** (in absence of alternative source, up to this limit is acceptable).
>
> **Five parameters with limits:**
>
> | Parameter | Desirable | Max Permissible |
> |-----------|-----------|-----------------|
> | pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation |
> | TDS | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L |
> | Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | 200 mg/L | 600 mg/L |
> | Nitrate (as NO₃⁻) | 45 mg/L | 100 mg/L |
> | Fluoride (F⁻) | 1.0 mg/L | 1.5 mg/L |
>
> **Note:** For microbiological parameters (E. coli, total coliforms), **zero** tolerance is permitted in any 100 mL sample.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choose either A or B**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model | Module 3, 14-Mark Standard]**

**(a)** A groundwater sample has the following ionic composition:

| Ion | Concentration (mg/L) |
|-----|---------------------|
| Na⁺ | 460 |
| K⁺ | 39 |
| Ca²⁺ | 200 |
| Mg²⁺ | 73 |
| Cl⁻ | 426 |
| SO₄²⁻ | 192 |
| HCO₃⁻ | 366 |

Calculate the **Sodium Adsorption Ratio (SAR)**, **Exchangeable Sodium Percentage (ESP)**, and **Residual Sodium Carbonate (RSC)**. Classify the water based on sodium hazard. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **WHO drinking water guidelines** for the following parameters with their health significance: (i) Arsenic, (ii) Fluoride, (iii) Nitrate, (iv) Lead, (v) E. coli. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

##### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1: Convert mg/L → meq/L for each ion.**

For Na⁺ (AW = 23.0, valency = 1):

$$[Na^+] = \frac{460 \times 1}{23.0} = 20.00 \text{ meq/L}$$

For K⁺ (AW = 39.10, valency = 1):

$$[K^+] = \frac{39 \times 1}{39.10} = 0.997 \text{ meq/L} \approx 1.00 \text{ meq/L}$$

For Ca²⁺ (AW = 40.08, valency = 2):

$$[Ca^{2+}] = \frac{200 \times 2}{40.08} = 9.98 \text{ meq/L}$$

For Mg²⁺ (AW = 24.30, valency = 2):

$$[Mg^{2+}] = \frac{73 \times 2}{24.30} = 6.01 \text{ meq/L}$$

For HCO₃⁻ (MW = 61.00, valency = 1):

$$[HCO_3^-] = \frac{366 \times 1}{61.00} = 6.00 \text{ meq/L}$$

**Step 2: Apply SAR formula.**

$$SAR = \frac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{\dfrac{[Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}]}{2}}} = \frac{20.00}{\sqrt{\dfrac{9.98 + 6.01}{2}}}$$

$$SAR = \frac{20.00}{\sqrt{\dfrac{15.99}{2}}} = \frac{20.00}{\sqrt{7.995}}$$

$$SAR = \frac{20.00}{2.827} = 7.075$$

$$\boxed{SAR = 7.08}$$

**Step 3: Compute ESP.**

$$ESP = 1.184 \times 7.08 - 1.82 = 8.383 - 1.82 = 6.56\%$$

$$\boxed{ESP = 6.56\%}$$

**Step 4: Compute RSC.**

$$RSC = (CO_3^{2-} + HCO_3^-) - (Ca^{2+} + Mg^{2+})$$

$$RSC = (0 + 6.00) - (9.98 + 6.01) = 6.00 - 15.99 = -9.99 \text{ meq/L}$$

$$\boxed{RSC = -9.99 \text{ meq/L (Safe, well below threshold)}}$$

**Step 5: Classify Sodium Hazard.**

| Parameter | Value | Class |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| SAR = 7.08 | < 10 | **S1 – Low Sodium Hazard** |
| ESP = 6.56% | < 15 | Safe |
| RSC = -9.99 | < 1.25 | Safe |

**Conclusion:** The water is **safe for irrigation** with respect to sodium-related hazards.

---

##### Part (b) — WHO Guidelines Discussion

| Parameter | WHO Limit | Health Significance |
|-----------|----------|---------------------|
| **(i) Arsenic (As)** | **0.01 mg/L (10 µg/L)** | Highly carcinogenic; causes skin lesions, lung, bladder, and kidney cancers; vascular diseases; long-term exposure even at low levels is hazardous. |
| **(ii) Fluoride (F⁻)** | **1.5 mg/L** | At low levels (<0.5 mg/L) prevents dental caries. Excess causes **dental fluorosis** (mottled teeth) and **skeletal fluorosis** (bone deformities, crippling). |
| **(iii) Nitrate (NO₃⁻)** | **50 mg/L** (acute, infants) | Causes **methemoglobinemia** ("blue baby syndrome") in infants; possible link to gastric cancer; indicates recent organic/fecal contamination. |
| **(iv) Lead (Pb)** | **0.01 mg/L (10 µg/L)** | **Neurotoxin**, particularly harmful to children (reduced IQ, learning disabilities); causes anemia, kidney damage, hypertension; bioaccumulative. |
| **(v) E. coli** | **0 per 100 mL** | Definitive indicator of **recent fecal contamination**; signals presence of enteric pathogens (*E. coli* O157:H7, *Salmonella*, *Shigella*, *Vibrio*, *Rotavirus*, *Giardia*, *Cryptosporidium*). |

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model | Module 3, 14-Mark Standard]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **biological health of groundwater**. Describe in detail the **Membrane Filtration (MF) method** and **Most Probable Number (MPN) method** for the enumeration of coliform bacteria. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **WHO** and **BIS (IS 10500:2012)** drinking water standards with respect to any five parameters. Discuss the role of the **ISI mark** in the Indian regulatory framework. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

##### Part (a) — Biological Health of Groundwater

**Concept:**

Biological health of groundwater refers to its microbiological quality concerning pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, helminths) that can cause waterborne diseases. Although groundwater is naturally protected by **soil filtration, aquifer matrix retention, and long subsurface travel times** (Darcy's natural purification), it is not sterile. Contamination pathways include:
- Septic tank leachate
- Landfill leachate
- Agricultural runoff (livestock waste, manure)
- Karst conduits (direct surface connection)
- Poorly constructed / damaged wells
- Floodwater intrusion

**Indicator Organisms:**
- **Total Coliforms:** Broad group of Gram-negative, lactose-fermenting rods; includes environmental species.
- **Fecal Coliforms:** Subgroup capable of growth at 44.5 °C; thermotolerant.
- **E. coli:** Definitive fecal indicator; specific to warm-blooded animal intestines.
- **Fecal Streptococci / Enterococci:** Complementary indicators, more resistant to chlorination.

**Detection Methods:**

**(i) Most Probable Number (MPN) Method:**

- Multi-tube **fermentation technique** using statistical estimation.
- Three dilutions × 5 tubes each (e.g., 10 mL, 1 mL, 0.1 mL) inoculated into **Lauryl Tryptose Broth (LTB)**.
- Incubated at **35 °C ± 0.5 °C for 24–48 h**.
- Positive tubes show **gas production** and/or **acid formation**.
- Confirmed positive tubes subcultured into **Brilliant Green Lactose Bile (BGLB) broth** at **44.5 °C ± 0.2 °C for 24 h** for fecal coliform confirmation.
- MPN/100 mL read from **standard statistical probability tables** (Hoskins formula).
- **Advantages:** Quantitative, statistically valid, works with turbid water.
- **Disadvantages:** Slow (48–96 h), wide confidence intervals.

**(ii) Membrane Filtration (MF) Method:**

- A measured volume (typically 100 mL) of water is filtered through a **sterile 0.45 µm membrane filter**.
- Filter placed on selective/differential medium:
  - **m-Endo Agar LES** → total coliforms (metallic sheen, 24 h, 35 °C).
  - **m-FC Agar** → fecal coliforms (blue colonies, 24 h, 44.5 °C).
- Colonies counted and reported as **CFU/100 mL** (Colony Forming Units).
- **Advantages:** Direct count, faster, lower detection limit, reproducible.
- **Disadvantages:** Unsuitable for turbid water (clogs membrane), high sediment/silt interferes.

**KTU Quick Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | MPN Method | MF Method |
|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Principle | Statistical estimation | Direct physical count |
| Result | MPN index/100 mL | CFU/100 mL |
| Time | 48–96 h | 24 h |
| Turbidity tolerance | High | Low |
| Precision | Lower (95% CI within ±1 log) | Higher |

---

##### Part (b) — Comparison of WHO vs BIS and Role of ISI

| Parameter | WHO (4th Ed., 2017) | BIS IS 10500:2012 (Desirable) | BIS IS 10500:2012 (Permissible) | Remarks |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------------|---------|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | 6.5 – 8.5 | No relaxation | Aligned |
| TDS | 1000 mg/L (max) | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L | BIS is more lenient at permissible limit |
| Hardness | 500 mg/L | 200 mg/L | 600 mg/L | BIS tighter at desirable; WHO no formal limit |
| Nitrate (NO₃⁻) | 50 mg/L (acute) | 45 mg/L | 100 mg/L | BIS stricter at desirable |
| Fluoride | 1.5 mg/L | 1.0 mg/L | 1.5 mg/L | BIS tighter |
| E. coli | 0/100 mL | 0/100 mL | 0/100 mL (no relaxation) | Identical |

**Role of ISI Mark:**

- **ISI** stands for **Indian Standards Institution**, established in 1947 (reconstituted in 1987 as **BIS**).
- The **ISI mark** on bottled water, water-treatment devices, pumps, and pipes is a **certification mark** indicating conformity to Indian Standards.
- Under the **Bureau of Indian Standards Act, 2016** (replacing the 1986 Act), the **ISI mark is now legally synonymous with the BIS Certification Mark**.
- For drinking water, the **ISI-certified bottled water** must conform to **IS 14543**, and packaged drinking water to **IS 10500**.
- In KTU exam answers, references to "ISI standard" should be treated as equivalent to BIS IS 10500:2012.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Forgetting to convert mg/L to meq/L before applying the SAR formula (this single error forfeits 3–4 marks).
> 2. **Atomic Weight Mismatch:** Using Na = 23.0 vs 22.99, or Mg = 24.3 vs 24.305 — even minor numerical errors cost a mark if final answer deviates >5%.
> 3. **Wrong Limits:** Writing Arsenic limit as 0.05 mg/L (old WHO 1993) instead of **0.01 mg/L** (current).
> 4. **Confusing ISI with BIS:** Stating them as separate, independent bodies. ISI = old name of BIS.
> 5. **Forgetting Carbonate in RSC:** Some students omit the CO₃²⁻ term — always write the **full expression** in your answer.
> 6. **Skipping Classification:** Computing SAR without classifying it into S1/S2/S3/S4 — always end numericals with explicit classification.
> 7. **MPN vs CFU:** Conflating the units (MPN is statistical; CFU is physical count).
> 8. **Forgetting Incubation Temperature:** MPN uses 35 °C for total coliforms and 44.5 °C for fecal — easy to forget.
> 9. **Skipping Significant Figures:** Always maintain 2–3 significant figures in final SAR/ESP values.
> 10. **No Conclusion Statement:** Always conclude the numerical problem with a clear sentence: *"The water is suitable/unsuitable for irrigation based on SAR/ESP/RSC analysis."*

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP] **Rapid Revision Checklist**

**SAR (Sodium Adsorption Ratio):**
- ✅ Dimensionless ratio — formula always uses **meq/L**.
- ✅ $SAR = \dfrac{[Na^+]}{\sqrt{([Ca^{2+}] + [Mg^{2+}])/2}}$
- ✅ Class S1 (<10), S2 (10–18), S3 (18–26), S4 (>26).
- ✅ Empirical correlation: **ESP = 1.184 × SAR − 1.82**.
- ✅ Pair SAR with **RSC** for comprehensive irrigation assessment.
- ✅ SAR > 26 → gypsum amendment recommended.

**Companion Indices (often paired with SAR in KTU questions):**
- ✅ **RSC** = (CO₃²⁻ + HCO₃⁻) − (Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺) in meq/L.
- ✅ **SSP** = Na ÷ Total Cations × 100%.
- ✅ **KR (Kelly's Ratio)** = Na ÷ (Ca + Mg); < 1 = suitable.
- ✅ **PI (Permeability Index)** — Doneen's classification.
- ✅ **MH (Magnesium Hazard)** = Mg ÷ (Ca + Mg) × 100%; > 50% = harmful.

**WHO Drinking Water Standards (current, 4th Ed.):**
- ✅ **Arsenic:** 0.01 mg/L (10 µg/L) — carcinogenic.
- ✅ **Fluoride:** 1.5 mg/L — fluorosis above; dental caries below 0.5 mg/L.
- ✅ **Nitrate:** 50 mg/L — methemoglobinemia in infants.
- ✅ **Lead:** 0.01 mg/L — neurotoxin, especially in children.
- ✅ **Cadmium:** 0.003 mg/L — renal damage.
- ✅ **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺):** 0.05 mg/L — carcinogenic.

**BIS IS 10500:2012 Standards:**
- ✅ Two-tier: **Desirable** + **Maximum Permissible**.
- ✅ **pH:** 6.5–8.5; **TDS:** 500 / 2000 mg/L; **Hardness:** 200 / 600 mg/L.
- ✅ **E. coli / Coliforms:** 0 per 100 mL (no relaxation).

**ISI ↔ BIS Equivalence:**
- ✅ **ISI = BIS** (renamed in 1987).
- ✅ ISI mark governed by **BIS Act, 2016**.

**Biological Health of Groundwater:**
- ✅ **E. coli** = definitive fecal indicator.
- ✅ **MPN** = statistical, multi-tube fermentation; 48–96 h.
- ✅ **MF** = direct count via membrane filter; 24 h.
- ✅ **Total Coliforms** at 35 °C; **Fecal Coliforms** at 44.5 °C.
- ✅ Common pathogens: *E. coli* O157:H7, *Salmonella typhi*, *Vibrio cholerae*, *Giardia lamblia*, *Cryptosporidium parvum*, Rotavirus.

**Conversion Mastery:**
- ✅ meq/L = (mg/L × valency) ÷ atomic weight.
- ✅ Reverse: mg/L = meq/L × atomic weight ÷ valency.

**Critical Examination Formulae:**
- ✅ Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃) = 2.5[Ca²⁺] + 4.12[Mg²⁺] in mg/L.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saline Water Intrusion in Coastal and Other Aquifers and Its Prevention, Ghyben-Herzberg Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET835 - HYDROGEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Saline Water Intrusion in Coastal and Other Aquifers and Its Prevention, Ghyben-Herzberg Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Saline Water Intrusion?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saline Water Intrusion (Saltwater Intrusion):** The landward migration of saline (sea) water into a freshwater aquifer, causing the displacement and eventual contamination of potable groundwater. It occurs wherever a hydraulic gradient favours the movement of denser saline water into a previously fresh groundwater system.

The intrusion manifests as a **dome or wedge-shaped body of salt water** advancing beneath the freshwater zone. Under undisturbed (pre-development) conditions, the freshwater maintains an equilibrium with the sea through a **sharp or diffuse interface**. Pumping, sea-level rise, reduced recharge, or channel modifications can break this equilibrium and drive the salt front inland/upward.

### 1.2 The Ghyben-Herzberg Relationship — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ghyben-Herzberg Relationship:** A hydrostatic equilibrium equation, independently proposed by **W. Badon-Ghyben (1888, Netherlands)** and **A. Herzberg (1889, North Sea Islands)**, that relates the **depth of the freshwater–seawater interface below mean sea level (MSL)** to the **elevation of the freshwater table above MSL**, based on the principle that a column of freshwater floats on the underlying denser seawater like a tall iceberg.

The classical form of the relationship is:

$$
z \;=\; \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\;\cdot h
$$

where:
- $z$ = depth of the freshwater–saltwater interface **below** MSL (m)
- $h$ = elevation of the **freshwater potentiometric surface** above MSL (m)
- $\rho_f$ = density of fresh groundwater (≈ **1000 kg/m³** or **1.000 g/cm³**)
- $\rho_s$ = density of seawater (≈ **1025 kg/m³** or **1.025 g/cm³**)

For typical seawater and freshwater densities, the constant simplifies to:

$$
\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \;=\; \frac{1.000}{1.025 - 1.000} \;=\; \frac{1.000}{0.025} \;=\; 40
$$

So the **practical rule of thumb** is:

$$
\boxed{\,z \;\approx\; 40\,h\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for students:** *"For every 1 m of freshwater head above the sea, there are 40 m of freshwater hidden beneath it."* This means the visible head is only ~2.4 % of the total freshwater thickness — a tiny change in $h$ translates into a massive change in $z$.

### 1.3 Other Aquifers Affected by Salinity

Saline water intrusion is **not restricted to coastal aquifers**. Salinity contamination also arises in:

1. **Inland (continental) aquifers** — through upward leakage of **connate (fossil) water** from deep sedimentary basins.
2. **Aquifers in arid / semi-arid regions** — through **irrigation return flow**, evapotranspiration concentrating salts, and **saline seeps**.
3. **Aquifers overlying evaporite deposits** — through **halite / gypsum dissolution** producing brine.
4. **Atoll and small island aquifers** — by saline intrusion from all sides under a thin freshwater lens.
5. **Estuarine / river-mouth aquifers** — by tidal saline wedges pushed inland.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Iceberg" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a freshwater aquifer as a **giant floating iceberg** with the visible top (the water table above sea level) representing only a sliver. The vast hidden mass beneath sea level is what sustains the freshwater reserve. Pumping from wells is like **sawing the iceberg from above**: the visible top sinks slowly, but the *hidden* bottom rises 40× faster — and once it passes the well screen, the well produces salt water.

A second useful analogy is a **ship floating in the sea**: the ship's height above water ($h$) is roughly **1/40th** of its draught below water ($z$). The freshwater aquifer behaves exactly like such a ship — but instead of buoyancy holding it up, **pressure equilibrium** with the underlying saltwater holds it together.

### 1.5 Standards, Constants & Reference Values Used in KTU Board Exams

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_f$ | **1.000** | g/cm³ |
| Density of seawater | $\rho_s$ | **1.025** | g/cm³ |
| Density ratio constant | $\rho_f / (\rho_s - \rho_f)$ | **40** | dimensionless |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **981** | cm/s² |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325** | kPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Examiners expect students to use $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³ and $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³ unless explicitly stated otherwise. Memorise the constant **40** — it is a frequent direct question.

## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hydrogeological Setting of a Coastal Aquifer

A **coastal aquifer** is a permeable geological formation (sand, gravel, fractured limestone, sandstone) that outcrops beneath the sea and extends inland. The freshwater it stores is **recharged inland** by rainfall and flows coastward under a natural hydraulic gradient. Because freshwater is **less dense** than seawater, it floats on top, forming a *freshwater lens* that thins towards the coast and pinches out at the submarine discharge zone.

The system comprises three key elements:
1. **Freshwater zone** — top, with $\rho_f \approx 1.000$ g/cm³.
2. **Saline water zone** — bottom, with $\rho_s \approx 1.025$ g/cm³.
3. **Interface (transition zone)** — between the two; may be **sharp** (idealised) or **diffuse** (real, due to dispersion & tidal mixing, typically 30–60 m thick).

### 2.2 Mechanism of Saline Intrusion — Why It Happens

Saline intrusion occurs whenever the **natural equilibrium** between fresh and salt water is disturbed. The driving forces include:

- **Groundwater withdrawal** (pumping wells lower $h$ ⇒ $z$ decreases ⇒ interface rises sharply).
- **Sea-level rise** (climate change, storm surges increase $\rho_s$ column ⇒ interface moves inland).
- **Reduction of natural recharge** (urbanisation, deforestation, drought).
- **Canal / drain seepage** introducing saline water from upstream.
- **Tidal oscillations** causing cyclic movement of the interface.
- **Geological changes** like faulting opening pathways to deeper saline aquifers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** Since $z = 40h$, a **drop of 1 m in $h$ causes a 40 m rise in the interface**. Hence, even modest over-pumping has devastating consequences for coastal aquifers.

### 2.3 Conditions for the Ghyben-Herzberg Relationship

The relationship is **derived under simplifying assumptions**:

1. **Static (hydrostatic) equilibrium** — no horizontal flow of seawater.
2. **Sharp interface** — no mixing / dispersion between fresh and salt water.
3. **Homogeneous, isotropic, unconfined aquifer** of uniform properties.
4. **Freshwater discharge** occurs only at the coastline (submarine outflow).
5. **Hydrostatic pressure distribution** on both sides of the interface.

These assumptions are reasonable for a **first-order estimate** but rarely hold in reality — hence the need for refined models (e.g., Hubbert's relation, sharp-interface models, dispersion models like Henry's problem).

### 2.4 Hubbert's Modification (1940)

Hubbert argued that if the aquifer is **confined** and the seawater column extends only to MSL (not to a deeper datum), the relation becomes:

$$
h \;=\; \frac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}\;\cdot z \;+\; \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_f}\;\cdot n
$$

where $n$ is the height of the tide (or sea-level fluctuation) above/below MSL.

Rearranging:

$$
z \;=\; \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\;\cdot h \;-\; \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\;\cdot n
$$

This **explicitly accounts for tidal influence** and is the version most often asked in KTU 14-mark problems.

### 2.5 Salt-Water Wedge Under Steady Flow — Glover Solution

When the freshwater flows coastward with a discharge $q_0$ per unit width, the interface no longer remains horizontal. It forms a **wedge** that approaches the sea asymptotically. The **Glover (1964) solution** gives the position of the interface measured from the inland reference point:

$$
z(x) \;=\; \sqrt{h_0^{\,2} \;-\; \frac{2\,q_0\,x}{\,K\,(\rho_s - \rho_f)/\rho_f\,}}
$$

At the toe of the wedge ($x = x_0$ where $z = 0$, the interface meets the sea at the outflow point):

$$
x_0 \;=\; \frac{K\,h_0^{\,2}\,(\rho_s - \rho_f)}{2\,q_0\,\rho_f}
$$

These are **high-yield KTU derivations** — board examiners frequently test both the Ghyben-Herzberg static case and the Glover dynamic case.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Examinations.** Master these — they account for > 80 % of numerical questions.

| # | Equation | Application | Required Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h$ | Depth of static interface below MSL (Ghyben-Herzberg) | $z, h, \rho_f, \rho_s$ | metres |
| 2 | $z \approx 40\,h$ | Simplified rule of thumb using standard densities | $z, h$ | metres |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}\,z + \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_f}\,n$ | Hubbert relation with tidal correction | $h, z, n, \rho_f, \rho_s$ | metres |
| 4 | $\Delta z = -\,40 \cdot \Delta h$ | Change in interface depth due to drawdown $\Delta h$ | $\Delta z, \Delta h$ | metres |
| 5 | $z(x) = \sqrt{h_0^{\,2} - \dfrac{2\,q_0\,x}{K\,(\rho_s - \rho_f)/\rho_f}}$ | Glover steady-flow wedge profile | $x, h_0, q_0, K$ | metres |
| 6 | $x_0 = \dfrac{K\,h_0^{\,2}\,(\rho_s - \rho_f)}{2\,q_0\,\rho_f}$ | Toe distance of wedge | $K, h_0, q_0, \rho_f, \rho_s$ | metres |
| 7 | $\tau = 0.35\,K\,i$ | Specific discharge from Darcy's Law | $K, i$ | m/day |
| 8 | $h_{crit} = \dfrac{Q}{2\,\pi\,T}\;\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)$ | Critical well head (prevention design) | $Q, T, R, r$ | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-symbol trap:** When typing $\Delta h$ or $\rho_s - \rho_f$ **inside a markdown table**, escape the absolute-value or ratio bar using $\mid$ or $\vert$. Some markdown renderers break tables if a raw $\vert$ appears inside a cell.

### 2.7 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Ghyben-Herzberg relationship is the **backbone of coastal groundwater management**. It is used by:
> - **Public health / municipal engineers** to locate safe well fields inland from the coast.
> - **Agricultural planners** in deltaic regions (e.g., Kerala, Bangladesh, Netherlands) to prevent saline soil salinisation.
> - **Island hydrogeologists** to design sustainable yield for atoll freshwater lenses.
> - **Climate adaptation planners** to project how sea-level rise will shrink coastal freshwater reserves.
> - **Numerical modellers** as the initial-condition analytical benchmark against which codes like MODFLOW-SWI, SEAWAT, and FEFLOW are validated.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Ghyben-Herzberg Static Equation (Full)

**Objective:** Find the depth $z$ below MSL of the interface in terms of the freshwater head $h$ above MSL.

#### Step 1 — Set up the hydrostatic column

Consider a vertical column of aquifer material extending from the freshwater water table down through the interface and into the underlying seawater. Let the freshwater table stand at a height $h$ above MSL, and let the interface lie at depth $z$ below MSL.

Therefore, the **freshwater column** has a total length of $(h + z)$, and the **seawater column** (from MSL down to the interface) has length $z$.

#### Step 2 — Write pressure at the interface from the freshwater side

The pressure at any depth in a static fluid column is $\rho\,g\,L$, where $L$ is the vertical length of the fluid column above that point.

Pressure at the interface due to the freshwater column:

$$
P_f \;=\; \rho_f\,g\,(h + z)
$$

#### Step 3 — Write pressure at the interface from the seawater side

The seawater column extends from MSL down to depth $z$:

$$
P_s \;=\; \rho_s\,g\,z
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the condition of hydrostatic equilibrium

At the interface, the pressures from the two sides must be equal (otherwise the interface would move):

$$
\rho_f\,g\,(h + z) \;=\; \rho_s\,g\,z
$$

#### Step 5 — Cancel $g$ (since $g \neq 0$)

$$
\rho_f\,(h + z) \;=\; \rho_s\,z
$$

#### Step 6 — Expand and collect $z$ terms

$$
\rho_f\,h \;+\; \rho_f\,z \;=\; \rho_s\,z
$$

$$
\rho_f\,h \;=\; (\rho_s - \rho_f)\,z
$$

#### Step 7 — Solve for $z$

$$
\boxed{\,z \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_f\,h}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,}
$$

This is the **Ghyben-Herzberg equation**.

#### Step 8 — Insert standard density values

Using $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³ and $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³:

$$
z \;=\; \frac{1000 \cdot h}{1025 - 1000} \;=\; \frac{1000\,h}{25} \;=\; 40\,h
$$

#### Step 9 — Sanity-check with units and intuition

- Units: $z$ (m) = (kg/m³ · m) / (kg/m³) = m. ✓
- For $h = 1$ m, $z = 40$ m. For $h = 0.5$ m, $z = 20$ m. ✓
- For $h = 0$, $z = 0$ (interface at sea level — physically correct when no freshwater column exists). ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners almost always award full marks if you explicitly state *"assuming hydrostatic equilibrium and a sharp interface"*. These two assumptions are the trigger phrases for the derivation.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hubbert's Modification Including Tidal Influence

**Objective:** Account for the fact that the sea level oscillates above or below MSL by an amount $n$.

#### Step 1 — Set up the new geometry

- MSL is the reference datum (zero).
- Instantaneous sea level = $n$ above MSL (positive during high tide, negative during low tide).
- Freshwater table is at height $h$ above MSL.
- Interface lies at depth $z$ below MSL.

The freshwater column above the interface is $(h + z)$ long. The seawater column above the interface is $(n + z)$ long.

#### Step 2 — Pressure equilibrium at the interface

$$
\rho_f\,g\,(h + z) \;=\; \rho_s\,g\,(n + z)
$$

#### Step 3 — Cancel $g$

$$
\rho_f\,(h + z) \;=\; \rho_s\,(n + z)
$$

#### Step 4 — Expand

$$
\rho_f\,h + \rho_f\,z \;=\; \rho_s\,n + \rho_s\,z
$$

#### Step 5 — Isolate $h$ (Hubbert's form)

$$
\rho_f\,h \;=\; (\rho_s - \rho_f)\,z + \rho_s\,n
$$

$$
h \;=\; \frac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}\,z + \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_f}\,n
$$

#### Step 6 — Alternative form, solving for $z$

$$
z \;=\; \frac{\rho_f\,h - \rho_s\,n}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \;=\; \frac{\rho_f\,h}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \;-\; \frac{\rho_s}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,n
$$

#### Step 7 — Numerical evaluation with standard densities

$$
\frac{\rho_s}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \;=\; \frac{1.025}{0.025} \;=\; 41
$$

So:

$$
\boxed{\,h \;=\; 40\,z \;+\; 41\,n\,} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad z \;=\; \frac{40\,h - 41\,n}{40 \cdot 41}\;... \text{(simplified numerically as } z \approx 40h - 41n\text{ for small } n\text{)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key physical insight from Hubbert:** When sea level rises ($n > 0$), the interface is pushed **deeper** (greater $z$) for the same $h$, because the heavier seawater column presses down on the interface. Conversely, during low tide, $z$ decreases.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Glover's Salt-Wedge Equation (Steady-State Outflow)

**Objective:** Find the steady-state position of the interface when freshwater discharges coastward at specific discharge $q_0$.

#### Step 1 — Apply Darcy's Law in the freshwater zone

Darcy's Law for unconfined flow:

$$
q(x) \;=\; -\,K\,h(x)\,\frac{dh}{dx}
$$

where $q(x)$ is the volumetric discharge per unit width at position $x$, $K$ is hydraulic conductivity, and $h(x)$ is the saturated freshwater thickness at position $x$.

#### Step 2 — At the interface, freshwater discharge equals $q_0$ (constant)

For steady-state, by continuity, $q(x) = q_0$ everywhere in the freshwater zone.

$$
q_0 \;=\; -\,K\,h(x)\,\frac{dh}{dx}
$$

#### Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate

Rearranging:

$$
-q_0\,\frac{dx}{K} \;=\; h\,dh
$$

Integrating from $x = 0$ (inland, where the freshwater table has elevation $h_0$ above MSL) to an arbitrary position $x$ (where the freshwater table is $h(x)$):

$$
\int_{0}^{x}\!\!-\frac{q_0}{K}\,dx \;=\; \int_{h_0}^{h(x)}\!\!h\,dh
$$

$$
-\frac{q_0\,x}{K} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\left[h(x)^{2} - h_0^{2}\right]
$$

$$
h(x)^{2} \;=\; h_0^{2} \;-\; \frac{2\,q_0\,x}{K}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert freshwater thickness to interface depth using Ghyben-Herzberg

For an unconfined coastal aquifer in dynamic equilibrium, the freshwater thickness equals the total head above the interface:

$$
h(x) \;=\; z(x) \cdot \frac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f} \;\;\approx\; \frac{z(x)}{40}
$$

Wait — careful: in Glover's setup the *freshwater column above the interface* extends from the freshwater table (at height $h$ above MSL) down to the interface (at depth $z$ below MSL). So:

$$
\text{Freshwater thickness} \;=\; h + z
$$

This is **not** the same as $h$ alone. The standard Glover derivation uses the freshwater-head $h(x)$ measured from the interface, in which case the Ghyben-Herzberg relation gives $z = 40 \cdot h(x)$ with $h(x)$ now being the total freshwater-column height. Renaming the freshwater head as $h_f$ for clarity:

$$
h_f(x) \;=\; h_0^{\,2} \;-\; \frac{2\,q_0\,x}{K}
$$

And the depth of the interface below MSL becomes:

$$
z(x) \;=\; \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\;h_f(x) \;\approx\; 40\,h_f(x)
$$

#### Step 5 — Final Glover wedge profile

$$
\boxed{\,z(x) \;=\; \sqrt{\,h_0^{\,2} \;-\; \dfrac{2\,q_0\,x}{K}\,\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,}\,}
$$

#### Step 6 — Distance from coastline to the inland toe

At the **toe of the wedge**, the interface intersects the sea floor (i.e., $z \to 0$). Setting $z = 0$ gives:

$$
0 \;=\; h_0^{\,2} \;-\; \frac{2\,q_0\,x_0}{K}\;\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}
$$

$$
x_0 \;=\; \frac{K\,h_0^{\,2}\,(\rho_s - \rho_f)}{2\,q_0\,\rho_f}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU exam note:** Glover's equation is rarely expected at full derivation. KTU boards typically accept the **final form** with a brief justification based on Darcy's Law and the Ghyben-Herzberg relation.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem:** In a coastal unconfined aquifer, the freshwater table stands **2.5 m above mean sea level**. Using the Ghyben-Herzberg relationship:
- (i) Calculate the depth of the freshwater–saltwater interface below MSL.
- (ii) If pumping causes the water table to drop by 0.8 m, what is the new interface depth and the rise of the interface?

**Solution (with explicit steps):**

**(i) Initial interface depth:**
Given: $h = 2.5$ m, $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³.

$$
z_1 \;=\; \frac{\rho_f\,h}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 2.5}{1025 - 1000} \;=\; \frac{2500}{25} \;=\; 100 \;\text{m}
$$

**Answer (i):** The interface is **100 m below MSL** initially.

**(ii) After 0.8 m drawdown:**

New freshwater head: $h_2 = 2.5 - 0.8 = 1.7$ m.

$$
z_2 \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 1.7}{25} \;=\; \frac{1700}{25} \;=\; 68 \;\text{m}
$$

**Rise of interface:**

$$
\Delta z \;=\; z_1 - z_2 \;=\; 100 - 68 \;=\; 32 \;\text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for valuation:** An 80 cm drop in head causes a **32 m rise of the salt-water front**. This is the dramatic amplification factor (40×) that makes coastal aquifers so vulnerable. Examiners *expect* a concluding remark like this for full marks.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Relation To Use When

| Condition / Problem Statement | Recommended Relation | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Unconfined aquifer, static equilibrium, simple problem | Ghyben-Herzberg ($z = 40h$) | Most basic, most tested |
| Confined aquifer, sea level varies (tides) | Hubbert's modification | Includes tidal term $n$ |
| Steady seaward flow, wedge geometry required | Glover's equation | Captures dynamic wedge |
| Mixing zone present (real aquifers) | Henry / dispersion model | Sharp-interface not valid |
| Inland (continental) saline intrusion | Vertical hydraulic gradient + connate water model | Static Ghyben-H. doesn't apply |

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]**
**Define the Ghyben-Herzberg relationship and state its significance in coastal hydrogeology.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Ghyben-Herzberg relationship** is a hydrostatic equilibrium equation that expresses the depth of the freshwater–seawater interface below mean sea level in terms of the freshwater head above mean sea level:

$$
z \;=\; \frac{\rho_f\,h}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \;\approx\; 40\,h
$$

where $\rho_f$ and $\rho_s$ are the densities of freshwater and seawater respectively.

**Significance:** (1) It is the **fundamental analytical tool** for evaluating the thickness of freshwater lenses in coastal and island aquifers. (2) It quantifies the **amplification effect** — a small drop in water-table head produces a much larger (40×) rise of the interface, explaining why coastal aquifers are highly vulnerable. (3) It is used as a **baseline for well-field design**, regulation of pumping, and policy decisions in coastal groundwater management.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]**
**List and briefly explain any four methods used to prevent saline water intrusion in coastal aquifers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 marks per method):**

1. **Reduction of pumping** — lowering the abstraction rate allows the water table to recover and the interface to retreat seaward.
2. **Relocation of wells** — shifting well fields inland away from the coast increases the natural freshwater buffer.
3. **Artificial recharge** — injecting surplus freshwater (or using spreading basins) raises the head $h$, pushing the interface deeper.
4. **Extraction barrier** — pumping a line of low-yield wells along the coast creates a *hydraulic trough* that intercepts the inland-moving salt front.
5. **Injection barrier** — injecting freshwater through coastal wells forms a *hydraulic ridge* that prevents the salt wedge from advancing.
6. **Subsurface (physical) barrier** — slurry walls or sheet-pile cut-offs block lateral saltwater movement.

*(Any four with one-line explanation = full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]**

**(a) Derive the Ghyben-Herzberg relationship for the depth of the freshwater-seawater interface below mean sea level in a coastal unconfined aquifer. State clearly the assumptions involved. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The aquifer is **homogeneous and isotropic** with a horizontal floor at the interface.
2. The freshwater and seawater are **separated by a sharp interface** (no mixing/dispersion).
3. **Hydrostatic equilibrium** prevails at the interface — both fluids are static.
4. The freshwater table has a **uniform horizontal surface** at height $h$ above MSL.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

At the interface, set up the pressure balance. Let the interface be at depth $z$ below MSL.

Freshwater column length above the interface = $h + z$.
Seawater column length above the interface (from MSL) = $z$.

Pressure at the interface from the freshwater side:

$$
P_f \;=\; \rho_f\,g\,(h + z)
$$

Pressure at the interface from the seawater side:

$$
P_s \;=\; \rho_s\,g\,z
$$

For hydrostatic equilibrium, $P_f = P_s$:

$$
\rho_f\,g\,(h + z) \;=\; \rho_s\,g\,z
$$

Cancel $g$ and rearrange:

$$
\rho_f\,h \;=\; (\rho_s - \rho_f)\,z
$$

$$
\boxed{\,z \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\;h\,}
$$

Substituting standard densities $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³ gives $z \approx 40\,h$.

---

**(b) Discuss the limitations of the Ghyben-Herzberg relationship. With the help of a sketch, describe the construction and working of an extraction barrier for saline intrusion prevention. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. Assumes a **sharp interface**, but in reality there is a **transition zone** (30–60 m thick) due to tidal mixing and dispersion.
2. Assumes **hydrostatic equilibrium**, ignoring seaward groundwater flow — Glover's dynamic equation is needed in flow cases.
3. **Does not account for tidal oscillations** — Hubbert's modification addresses this.
4. Assumes **homogeneous, isotropic aquifer**; real aquifers are stratified and anisotropic.
5. **Density values are assumed constant** with depth, salinity, and temperature — but density varies in estuarine and brackish systems.
6. Cannot handle **pumping-induced cones of depression** around wells — requires numerical modelling (MODFLOW-SWI / SEAWAT).

**Extraction Barrier (4 marks):**

An **extraction barrier** is a line of low-capacity pumping wells installed between the main freshwater well field and the coastline.

**Construction:**
- A series of shallow, small-capacity wells (typically 25–50 mm dia., 5–15 m deep) drilled in a line parallel to the coast, 50–200 m inland from the shoreline.
- Connected to a common sump / drainage system for disposal of saline water.

**Working:**
- Continuous pumping from these wells creates a **hydraulic trough** (localised cone of depression) along the coast.
- This trough **reverses the natural landward hydraulic gradient** at the barrier, preventing the salt-water wedge from migrating inland.
- The barrier wells pump slightly saline water, which is discharged safely (e.g., to sea or evaporation pond).
- The main freshwater well field, located further inland, continues to operate safely.

**Sketch description (for hand-drawn answer):**
- Draw MSL as a horizontal datum.
- Draw landward well field (main supply wells) inland.
- Draw extraction barrier wells (smaller circles) in a line parallel to coast between main wells and sea.
- Indicate water table: high inland, dipping into a trough at the barrier, then rising slightly near coast.
- Indicate salt-water interface sloping upward landward, intercepted at the barrier.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — Model Question Bank]**

**(a) Explain the concept of saline water intrusion in coastal aquifers. Discuss the various natural and anthropogenic factors that cause saline intrusion. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Saline water intrusion is the **landward migration of seawater into a coastal freshwater aquifer**, replacing freshwater in the pore spaces. It occurs because freshwater ($\rho \approx 1.000$ g/cm³) is less dense than seawater ($\rho \approx 1.025$ g/cm³), so the freshwater **floats on top** in a layer that thickens inland and pinches out at the coastline. Under natural equilibrium, this configuration is stable; disturbance causes the salt front to move.

The interface position is described by the **Ghyben-Herzberg relationship**:

$$
z \;=\; 40\,h
$$

where $h$ is the freshwater head above MSL and $z$ is the interface depth below MSL.

**Natural Factors (2 marks):**
1. **Sea-level rise** due to climate change / storm surges.
2. **Tidal oscillations** causing cyclic interface movement.
3. **Drought** reducing natural recharge.
4. **Geological faulting / fracturing** opening pathways to deeper saline formations.
5. **Tsunamis / storm surges** causing sudden infiltration of saline water.

**Anthropogenic Factors (2 marks):**
1. **Excessive groundwater pumping** (the dominant cause globally) — lowers $h$, raises $z$.
2. **Reduction of recharge** due to urbanisation (impervious surfaces), deforestation.
3. **Canal and drain seepage** introducing saline water.
4. **Coastal land-use changes** (destruction of dunes, mangroves, recharge zones).
5. **Mining / quarrying near coast** altering aquifer geometry.

---

**(b) Describe in detail the various methods used for the prevention and control of saline water intrusion in coastal aquifers. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Reduction / Regulation of Pumping (1 mark)**
Imposing limits on groundwater withdrawal through licensing; rotational pumping; declaring critical zones.

**2. Relocation of Well Fields (1 mark)**
Moving well fields inland, away from the coast, to maintain a thick freshwater buffer.

**3. Artificial Recharge (1.5 marks)**
- *Spreading method* — water spread in infiltration basins.
- *Injection method* — pumped through recharge wells.
- *ASR (Aquifer Storage and Recovery)* — store treated freshwater in the aquifer during wet season.
- Raises $h$, depresses the interface.

**4. Extraction Barrier (1 mark)**
A line of low-yield pumping wells near the coast creating a hydraulic trough that intercepts the salt wedge.

**5. Injection Barrier (1 mark)**
A line of injection wells along the coast forming a freshwater hydraulic ridge (pressure mound) that prevents inland migration.

**6. Subsurface Physical Barrier (1 mark)**
Impermeable cut-off walls (slurry walls, sheet piling) driven into the aquifer perpendicular to the coast to physically block lateral flow.

**7. Combination (Hybrid) Methods (0.5 mark)**
e.g., extraction barrier + artificial recharge; or injection barrier + physical barrier.

**8. Land-use Management & Policy (0.5 mark)**
Protecting recharge zones, regulating coastal construction, managed aquifer recharge policy, community awareness.

**Conclusion:**
The most effective strategy combines **regulatory**, **engineering**, and **ecological** measures. Numerical groundwater models (MODFLOW, SEAWAT, FEFLOW) are essential for designing and optimising these interventions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** before the Ghyben-Herzberg derivation. KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** purely for listing *sharp interface*, *hydrostatic equilibrium*, and *homogeneous aquifer*. Many students omit this and lose easy marks.
>
> 2. **Wrong sign convention** in Hubbert's modification. Students confuse whether $n$ is added or subtracted during high tide. **Mnemonic:** *"High tide presses the interface deeper"* ⇒ $n$ enters with a positive sign on the seawater side.
>
> 3. **Units slip-ups** in numerical problems. Always convert $\rho_f$ to kg/m³ (not g/cm³) when using $g$ = 9.81 m/s², or to g/cm³ (not kg/m³) when using $g$ = 981 cm/s². Mixing units silently = **zero marks** despite correct formula.
>
> 4. **Misreading the question** — confusing *"drawdown"* with *"recharge"*. Drawdown reduces $h$, increasing $z$; recharge does the opposite. A single sign error can change the answer by **40× the head change**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the diagram.** KTU Part B questions that ask *"with a neat sketch"* expect a labelled diagram. Missing the diagram = **2–3 mark penalty**, even with correct text.
>
> 6. **Listing prevention methods without explaining their working principle.** Examiners want the *mechanism* (e.g., *"extraction barrier creates a hydraulic trough"*) — not just the name.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Saline Water Intrusion & Ghyben-Herzberg Relationship**

**Core Concepts:**
- [x] Saline intrusion = landward migration of seawater into freshwater aquifer.
- [x] Density of freshwater $\rho_f = 1.000$ g/cm³; seawater $\rho_s = 1.025$ g/cm³.
- [x] Density ratio constant = **40** (memorise!).
- [x] Ghyben (1888, Netherlands) and Herzberg (1889, North Sea Islands) — independent discovery.
- [x] Sharp interface vs. transition zone — Ghyben-Herzberg uses sharp interface.

**Key Equations:**
- [x] $z = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h \;\;\approx\;\; 40\,h$
- [x] Hubbert: $h = \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}\,z + \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_f}\,n$
- [x] Glover wedge: $z(x) = \sqrt{h_0^{\,2} - \dfrac{2\,q_0\,x}{K}\dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}}$
- [x] Toe distance: $x_0 = \dfrac{K\,h_0^{\,2}(\rho_s - \rho_f)}{2\,q_0\,\rho_f}$
- [x] Amplification: $\Delta z = -40\,\Delta h$

**Assumptions of Ghyben-Herzberg:**
- [x] Sharp interface, hydrostatic equilibrium, homogeneous & isotropic aquifer, no horizontal seawater flow, constant densities.

**Prevention Methods:**
- [x] **Reduce pumping** — lower abstraction.
- [x] **Relocate wells** — move inland.
- [x] **Artificial recharge** — increase $h$.
- [x] **Extraction barrier** — coastal pumping creates hydraulic trough.
- [x] **Injection barrier** — freshwater ridge along coast.
- [x] **Physical barrier** — slurry walls, sheet piles.
- [x] **Land-use controls** — protect recharge zones.

**Other Aquifers Affected:**
- [x] Inland aquifers with **connate water** leakage.
- [x] **Arid-zone** saline seeps and irrigation return flow.
- [x] **Atoll** freshwater lenses.
- [x] **Estuarine** tidal aquifers.

**Critical Numerical Insights:**
- [x] 1 m drop in $h$ ⇒ 40 m rise in $z$ (drastic amplification).
- [x] Hubbert's tidal constant = **41** (not 40).
- [x] Glover's equation valid only for steady seaward flow.

**Exam Strategy:**
- [x] Always state assumptions before deriving Ghyben-Herzberg.
- [x] Always sketch the system with MSL, $h$, $z$ clearly labelled.
- [x] Use $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³ unless problem states otherwise.
- [x] Mention real-world context (Kerala coast, atolls, sea-level rise) for bonus marks.
- [x] Conclude with practical management implication (e.g., *"Hence a regulated pumping policy is essential"*).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods and Need for Artificial Recharge to Aquifers, Groundwater Management, Groundwater Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET835 - HYDROGEOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methods and Need for Artificial Recharge to Aquifers, Groundwater Management, Groundwater Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Artificial Recharge of Aquifers — Formal Definition

**Artificial Recharge (AR)** is the deliberate, engineered process of augmenting the natural replenishment of groundwater storage in an aquifer by introducing surplus surface water (rivers, canals, tanks, harvested rainwater, treated wastewater) into the subsurface geological formations through designed structures such as recharge wells, spreading basins, or injection systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** *"Artificial recharge is the process by which the groundwater reservoir is augmented at a rate exceeding that of natural replenishment by artificial means."*

The governing principle is rooted in the **hydrological cycle**: surface water is intercepted, conveyed, and forced to infiltrate downward through the **unsaturated zone (vadose zone)** until it reaches the **saturated zone (phreatic zone)**, thereby raising the water table.

## 1.2 The Banking Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

Imagine the **aquifer as a savings bank account** and **recharge as the deposit**:

- **Natural recharge** (rainfall infiltration) = Your monthly salary credit. It is small, irregular, and slow.
- **Groundwater draft** (pumping from wells) = Your monthly expenses. It can be large and frequent.
- **Artificial recharge** = A **lump-sum deposit** you make to top up the account when expenses exceed income.
- **Over-pumping without recharge** = Continuous deficit spending, leading to **bankruptcy of the aquifer** — manifested as falling water tables, land subsidence, and saline intrusion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** When withdrawal rates (Q_out) exceed natural recharge (Q_in) for prolonged periods, the aquifer is in a state of **groundwater mining**. AR is the engineered intervention to restore the **dynamic equilibrium** of the storage system.

## 1.3 Need for Artificial Recharge — Engineering Rationale

The need for artificial recharge arises from a clear imbalance in the **groundwater budget equation**:

$$\Delta S = (P + I_r + I_{ar}) - (ET + Q_d + Q_{of})$$

Where:
- $\Delta S$ = Change in groundwater storage
- $P$ = Precipitation contribution
- $I_r$ = Natural recharge from infiltration
- $I_{ar}$ = Artificial recharge input
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration loss
- $Q_d$ = Groundwater draft (pumping)
- $Q_{of}$ = Subsurface outflow

When $\Delta S < 0$ for sustained periods, AR becomes essential.

### 1.3.1 Primary Reasons for Artificial Recharge (KTU High-Yield Points)

1. **Augmenting groundwater storage** in depleted aquifers (urban and rural).
2. **Restoring water table levels** that have declined due to over-extraction.
3. **Improving groundwater quality** by dilution of contaminated plumes.
4. **Mitigating saltwater intrusion** in coastal aquifers by maintaining a positive hydraulic gradient seaward.
5. **Subsidence control** — preventing land subsidence caused by compaction of aquifer matrix.
6. **Storing surface water** in subsurface reservoirs for later use (cheaper than surface dams).
7. **Maintaining environmental flows** in rivers and springs during dry seasons.
8. **Mitigating drought impacts** and ensuring water security.
9. **Recharging deeper confined aquifers** where natural recharge is negligible.
10. **Sustainable management of watershed** as a holistic resource system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** The question *"Discuss the need for artificial recharge"* is a **guaranteed 7-marker** in KTU End Semester Examinations. Always structure the answer around the **groundwater budget equation** and cite **at least 5 practical scenarios** (e.g., Kochi coastal belt, Palakkad gap, Bengaluru urban).

## 1.4 Groundwater Management — Definition

**Groundwater Management** is the planned, scientific, and regulatory approach to control the development, utilization, conservation, and protection of groundwater resources to ensure their long-term sustainability and ecological balance.

It operates on three pillars:
- **Assessment** (quantifying the resource)
- **Regulation** (legal and policy frameworks)
- **Conservation** (efficiency, recycling, AR)

## 1.5 Groundwater Development — Definition

**Groundwater Development** refers to the engineering activities undertaken to harness, extract, and utilize groundwater for beneficial purposes — including construction of wells, boreholes, pumps, and associated distribution networks.

The development potential is governed by the **Safe Yield ($S_y$)**, which is the maximum rate at which water can be withdrawn from an aquifer indefinitely without causing unacceptable depletion.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Artificial Recharge Methods

Artificial Recharge Methods are broadly classified into **Direct Methods** and **Indirect Methods**, with further sub-classifications as per the **Central Ground Water Board (CGWB)** and **Ministry of Jal Shakti, Government of India** standards.

### 2.1.1 Direct Methods of Artificial Recharge

These methods introduce surface water directly into the aquifer at the point of application.

#### A. Spreading Methods (Surface Spreading)
Suitable for **unconfined aquifers** with permeable surficial soils.

1. **Flooding / Inundation Method**
   - Water is released from canals and allowed to flood a large flat area.
   - Best for deep, permeable soils overlying the aquifer.
   - **Limitation:** High water loss due to evaporation; requires large land area.

2. **Recharge Basin / Percolation Pond**
   - Engineered basin (depth 1–3 m) where water is impounded and allowed to percolate.
   - **Design rule:** Basin area = 10–15% of catchment area for clayey soils; 5–8% for sandy soils.
   - Periodic desilting is essential.

3. **Stream Channel Modification / Check Dams**
   - Small barriers constructed across ephemeral streams to slow flow and enhance infiltration.
   - **Cost-effective** in rural watersheds.

4. **Ditch / Furrow Recharge**
   - Parallel shallow trenches (depth 0.5–1.5 m) dug along contours; water diverted into them.
   - Used in agricultural fields.

5. **Terracing and Contour Bunding**
   - Mechanical measure where terraces interrupt overland flow, increasing residence time and infiltration.

#### B. Sub-Surface / Injection Methods
Suitable for **confined aquifers** or where surface spreading is infeasible (urban areas, impervious cover).

1. **Recharge Wells / Injection Wells**
   - Vertical drilled wells (depth 30–300 m) into the target aquifer.
   - **Three types:**
     - **Class I — Dry Well:** Water injected into dry portion of well.
     - **Class II — Perforated / Gravel-Packed Well:** Water enters through perforations across saturated zone.
     - **Class III — Open-Bottom Well:** Water enters from the bottom (for confined aquifers).
   - **Efficiency:** 50–90% of injected water reaches the aquifer.

2. **Recharge Shafts**
   - Vertical shafts (depth 5–15 m, diameter 1–3 m) filled with coarse media.
   - Used to recharge aquifers underlying clay layers.

3. **Recharge Pits and Trenches**
   - Excavated pits (depth 2–5 m) filled with boulder-gravel-sand layers.
   - Suitable for recharging shallow aquifers in urban housing colonies.

4. **Induced Recharge**
   - Natural recharge from a surface water body induced by pumping a nearby well, creating a hydraulic gradient.

### 2.1.2 Indirect Methods of Artificial Recharge

These do not directly inject water but augment natural recharge by altering subsurface conditions.

1. **Groundwater Dams / Sub-Surface Dams**
   - Underground barriers built across aquifers to arrest subsurface flow and raise water table.
   - Highly effective in hard rock terrains (laterite, basalt).

2. **Dug Well Recharge**
   - Existing open wells are used to recharge by desilting and connecting rooftop or surface runoff.

3. **Sand Dam** (used in Kenya and semi-arid India)
   - A dam constructed across a sandy riverbed; water stored within the sand matrix by seasonal replenishment.

4. **Modification of Irrigation Practices**
   - Laser leveling, drip irrigation, and sprinkler systems reduce runoff and enhance in-situ recharge.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for the KTU End Semester Examination. Memorize the variable definitions and units to avoid mark deductions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | Darcy's Law: $Q$ = discharge, $K$ = hydraulic conductivity, $i$ = hydraulic gradient, $A$ = cross-sectional area | Foundation equation for all groundwater flow analysis |
| 2 | $Q_r = P_r \cdot A \cdot t$ | Recharge volume: $Q_r$ = total recharge, $P_r$ = recharge rate (m/day), $A$ = area, $t$ = time | Computing basin water storage capacity |
| 3 | $P_r = K \cdot i = K \cdot \left(\frac{h_1 - h_2}{L}\right)$ | Recharge rate per unit area | Spread/percolation method design |
| 4 | $\Delta h = \frac{Q_{inj}}{4 \pi T} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Rise in water table due to injection well: $\Delta h$ = head change, $Q_{inj}$ = injection rate, $T$ = transmissivity, $r_1$, $r_2$ = radial distances | Sizing recharge well networks |
| 5 | $T = K \cdot b$ | Transmissivity: $b$ = aquifer thickness | Confined aquifer analysis |
| 6 | $S = S_y \cdot A$ | Storage volume: $S$ = groundwater storage, $S_y$ = specific yield | Estimating recoverable reserve |
| 7 | $V_{res} = A \cdot b \cdot S_y$ | Volume of recoverable groundwater reserve | Aquifer storage calculation |
| 8 | $Q_s = A \cdot N \cdot F$ | Safe yield estimation: $Q_s$ = safe yield, $A$ = area, $N$ = annual precipitation, $F$ = factor of recharge | Master planning of water supply |
| 9 | $RF = \frac{R_{eff}}{R_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Recharge factor / efficiency | Evaluating AR scheme performance |
| 10 | $K = \frac{\mu}{\gamma} \cdot k$ | Hydraulic conductivity relation to permeability: $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity, $\gamma$ = specific weight, $k$ = intrinsic permeability | Petrophysical correlations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Table Note (Per KTU Directive):** All absolute value notations use `\vert` to prevent markdown parser conflicts.

## 2.3 Selection Criteria for Artificial Recharge Method (Engineering Logic)

The choice of an AR technique is governed by:

- **Hydrogeological conditions:** Unconfined vs. confined aquifer; depth to water table; aquifer thickness; permeability.
- **Availability of source water:** Seasonal surplus from rivers, canals, stormwater, or treated wastewater.
- **Topography and land use:** Sufficient land area for spreading; urban density forcing injection methods.
- **Quality of source water:** Suspended solids in spreading basins cause clogging; turbidity < 50 NTU preferred.
- **Cost-benefit ratio:** Spreading methods are cheaper (₹15–50 lakh per cumec of recharge) than deep injection wells (₹80–200 lakh per cumec).
- **Climate and evaporation regime:** In arid zones, sub-surface methods preferred to minimize evaporative loss.

## 2.4 Groundwater Management — Theoretical Framework

### 2.4.1 The Dynamic Equilibrium Concept

A sustainable groundwater regime requires that the **long-term average extraction does not exceed the long-term average recharge**:

$$\int_{0}^{T} Q_{in}\,dt = \int_{0}^{T} Q_{out}\,dt + \text{Natural Discharge}$$

### 2.4.2 Management Tools

1. **Hydrogeological Mapping** — Geological Survey of India (GSI) and CGWB maps.
2. **Numerical Modeling** — MODFLOW, FEFLOW for flow and transport simulation.
3. **Monitoring Networks** — Observation wells and piezometers for water level trends.
4. **Legal Framework** — Kerala Ground Water (Regulation and Control of Development and Management) Act, 2022.
5. **Demand-Side Management** — Micro-irrigation, rainwater harvesting, recycling.

### 2.4.3 Groundwater Quality Management

- **Salinity control** through dilution and prevention of seawater intrusion.
- **Contamination remediation** — Pump-and-treat, in-situ bioremediation.
- **Fluoride and nitrate mitigation** — Ion exchange, activated alumina, reverse osmosis.

## 2.5 Groundwater Development — Engineering Parameters

| Parameter | Definition | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Capacity** | $S_c = Q / s_d$ (discharge per unit drawdown) | m³/hr/m or lpm/m |
| **Well Efficiency** | $\eta_w = (S_c)_{actual} / (S_c)_{theoretical} \times 100$ | % |
| **Transmissivity (T)** | Capacity of aquifer to transmit water | m²/day |
| **Specific Yield (S_y)** | Volume of water drained per unit area per unit decline | dimensionless (fraction) |
| **Storativity (S)** | Volume released per unit area per unit decline in confined aquifers | dimensionless |
| **Safe Yield** | Annual extraction sustainable without depletion | Mm³/year |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Recharge Volume in a Spreading Basin

### Problem Statement:
A recharge basin of rectangular shape with length $L$ (m) and width $W$ (m) is flooded to a depth $d$ (m). The soil has an infiltration capacity governed by Horton's equation. Derive the expression for total recharge over time $t$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

The instantaneous infiltration rate $f(t)$ according to Horton's model is:

$$f(t) = f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \cdot e^{-k_h t}$$

Where:
- $f_c$ = final steady infiltration capacity (m/hr)
- $f_0$ = initial infiltration capacity (m/hr)
- $k_h$ = Horton's decay constant (1/hr)

The **cumulative infiltration depth** $F(t)$ (m) is obtained by integrating $f(t)$ from $0$ to $t$:

$$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau) \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} \left[ f_c + (f_0 - f_c) \cdot e^{-k_h \tau} \right] d\tau$$

Breaking the integral into two parts:

$$F(t) = f_c \int_{0}^{t} d\tau + (f_0 - f_c) \int_{0}^{t} e^{-k_h \tau} \, d\tau$$

The first integral evaluates to:

$$f_c \int_{0}^{t} d\tau = f_c \cdot t$$

The second integral evaluates to (using $\int e^{-k_h \tau} d\tau = -\frac{1}{k_h} e^{-k_h \tau}$):

$$(f_0 - f_c) \left[ -\frac{1}{k_h} e^{-k_h \tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = (f_0 - f_c) \cdot \frac{1}{k_h} \left( 1 - e^{-k_h t} \right)$$

Combining both:

$$F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k_h} \left( 1 - e^{-k_h t} \right)$$

The **total recharge volume** $V_r$ (m³) over the basin area $A = L \times W$ is:

$$V_r = A \cdot F(t) = L \cdot W \cdot \left[ f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k_h} \left( 1 - e^{-k_h t} \right) \right]$$

### Physical Interpretation:
- The first term $f_c \cdot t$ represents the **long-term steady-state** recharge.
- The second term is the **early-time excess** due to high initial infiltration.
- This derivation is essential for designing recharge basins with silting control.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drawdown-Rise Equation for an Injection Well

### Problem Statement:
Water is injected at a constant rate $Q_{inj}$ into a fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer. Derive the **Thiem-type equation** for the rise of piezometric head.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

For steady-state radial flow toward (or in this case, away from) a well, Darcy's Law in cylindrical coordinates is:

$$Q = -K \cdot A(r) \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}$$

For a cylindrical surface at radius $r$:

$$A(r) = 2 \pi r \cdot b$$

where $b$ is the aquifer thickness. Substituting:

$$Q = -K \cdot (2 \pi r b) \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}$$

Rearranging variables (and noting that for an injection well, the sign convention reverses because head increases outward):

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = -2 \pi K b \cdot dh$$

Integrating from radius $r_1$ (head $h_1$) to $r_2$ (head $h_2$):

$$Q \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r} = -2 \pi K b \int_{h_1}^{h_2} dh$$

$$Q \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = -2 \pi K b (h_2 - h_1) = 2 \pi K b (h_1 - h_2)$$

Defining transmissivity $T = K b$:

$$Q \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = 2 \pi T (h_1 - h_2)$$

The rise in head $\Delta h = h_1 - h_2$ is therefore:

$$\Delta h = \frac{Q}{2 \pi T} \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

This is the **fundamental recharge well design equation** used to predict the cone of impression (analogous to the cone of depression in pumping wells).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the factor of $2\pi$ instead of $4\pi$. For 2D radial flow (plan view) without partial penetration, it is $2\pi T$. For a fully 3D analysis, $4\pi T$ may apply. Always check the aquifer type.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Safe Yield Using the Water Budget Approach

The water budget for a watershed area $A$ over a year is:

$$S = P - R - ET - D + G_{in} - G_{out} \pm \Delta G$$

Where:
- $S$ = Surplus available for AR
- $P$ = Precipitation (mm/yr)
- $R$ = Surface runoff (mm/yr)
- $ET$ = Evapotranspiration (mm/yr)
- $D$ = Deep percolation (mm/yr, natural)
- $G_{in}, G_{out}$ = Groundwater inflow/outflow
- $\Delta G$ = Change in groundwater storage

For a **closed basin** (no significant $G_{in}$ or $G_{out}$), the **safe yield** $Q_s$ in m³/year is:

$$Q_s = A \cdot R_f \cdot P - Q_{existing}$$

Where $R_f$ is the recharge factor (0.10–0.20 for alluvial aquifers, 0.05–0.10 for hard rock).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of AR Methods

| Method | Aquifer Type | Land Area | Capital Cost | Recharge Efficiency | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Recharge Basin | Unconfined | High | Low | 40–70% | Rural watersheds |
| Percolation Tank | Unconfined (semi-arid) | High | Low–Medium | 30–60% | Maharashtra, Karnataka dry zones |
| Recharge Well | Confined or deep unconfined | Negligible | High | 50–90% | Urban areas, clay cover |
| Recharge Shaft | Confined with clay cap | Small | Medium | 60–85% | Indo-Gangetic plains |
| Induced Recharge | All types | Medium | Medium | 70–90% | Riverbank filtration |
| Check Dam | Unconfined shallow | Small | Very Low | 25–50% | Ephemeral streams |
| Sand Dam | Unconfined | Small | Low | 60–80% | Semi-arid sandy rivers |
| Sub-Surface Dam | Hard rock | Small | Medium | 70–90% | Basaltic and lateritic terrain |

## 3.5 Numerical Example — KTU Style (Worked Out)

**Question:** A recharge basin of dimensions $50 \text{ m} \times 30 \text{ m}$ is filled with water to a depth of 1.2 m. The infiltration rate at the start is 1.5 m/day, the final steady rate is 0.3 m/day, and the Horton's constant is 0.8 day⁻¹. Calculate the cumulative recharge volume over 5 days, assuming no evaporation.

### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- $L = 50$ m, $W = 30$ m, $A = 1500$ m²
- $f_0 = 1.5$ m/day, $f_c = 0.3$ m/day, $k_h = 0.8$ day⁻¹
- $t = 5$ days

**Step 2:** Apply Horton's equation for cumulative infiltration.

$$F(t) = f_c \cdot t + \frac{(f_0 - f_c)}{k_h} \left( 1 - e^{-k_h t} \right)$$

$$F(5) = (0.3)(5) + \frac{(1.5 - 0.3)}{0.8} \left( 1 - e^{-0.8 \times 5} \right)$$

$$F(5) = 1.5 + \frac{1.2}{0.8} \left( 1 - e^{-4.0} \right)$$

$$e^{-4.0} \approx 0.01832$$

$$F(5) = 1.5 + 1.5 \times (1 - 0.01832) = 1.5 + 1.5 \times 0.98168$$

$$F(5) = 1.5 + 1.4725 = 2.9725 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Total recharge volume.

$$V_r = A \cdot F(5) = 1500 \times 2.9725 = 4458.75 \text{ m}^3 \approx 4459 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Verify against initial water depth.
- Initial storage = $1500 \times 1.2 = 1800$ m³.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The cumulative recharge (4459 m³) **exceeds the initial storage** (1800 m³). This implies continuous water supply is needed — the basin cannot be filled once; it requires a **continuous inflow** to sustain recharge. This is a classic KTU trap question.
